kids.question: Who was speaking to the Israelites in Deuteronomy chapter 1?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:1 — "These be the words which Moses spake unto all Israel."

kids.question: Where were the Israelites camped when Moses gave this speech?
kids.option_a: In the wilderness on the other side of Jordan
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:1 — "These be the words which Moses spake unto all Israel on this side Jordan in the wilderness."

kids.question: Moses sent men to spy out the land. What land were they spying?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:22 — "Let us send men before us, and they shall search us out the land."

teens.question: What were the judges Moses appointed instructed to do?
teens.option_a: Hear causes between brethren and judge righteously
teens.option_b: Collect taxes for the tabernacle
teens.option_c: Teach the law in every city
teens.option_d: Lead the army into battle
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:16 — "Hear the causes between your brethren, and judge righteously between every man and his brother."

teens.question: What did the ten unfaithful spies say about the people of Canaan?
teens.option_a: They were greater and taller than us and their cities walled up to heaven
teens.option_b: They were friendly and would share the land
teens.option_c: They were few in number and easily defeated
teens.option_d: They had already fled from Israel
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:28 — "The people is greater and taller than we; the cities are great and walled up to heaven."

teens.question: Which two spies gave a good report and remained faithful?
teens.option_a: Caleb and Joshua
teens.option_b: Moses and Aaron
teens.option_c: Reuben and Gad
teens.option_d: Phinehas and Eleazar
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:36-38 — Caleb wholly followed the LORD, and Joshua the son of Nun shall go in.

teens.question: What did God say would happen to the faithless generation?
teens.option_a: They would wander forty years and not see the promised land
teens.option_b: They would be enslaved again in Egypt
teens.option_c: They would be destroyed immediately
teens.option_d: They would be scattered among other nations
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:35 — "Surely there shall not one of these men of this evil generation see that good land."

teens.question: When Israel attempted to go up and fight after being told they could not, what happened?
teens.option_a: The Amorites chased them and smote them in Seir
teens.option_b: They won a great victory
teens.option_c: God sent fire to stop them
teens.option_d: The Canaanites surrendered
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:44 — "And the Amorites...came out against you, and chased you, as bees do, and destroyed you in Seir."

adults.question: In what year after leaving Egypt did Moses begin his address in Deuteronomy 1?
adults.option_a: The fortieth year, in the eleventh month
adults.option_b: The twentieth year
adults.option_c: The thirtieth year
adults.option_d: The thirty-eighth year
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:3 — "And it came to pass in the fortieth year, in the eleventh month...that Moses spake."

adults.question: What was Moses' complaint about judging the people alone?
adults.option_a: He could not bear the burden alone because God had multiplied them as the stars of heaven
adults.option_b: He was too old to hear all cases
adults.option_c: The people refused to obey his rulings
adults.option_d: He lacked wisdom to decide difficult matters
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:9-10 — "I am not able to bear you myself alone...the LORD your God hath multiplied you, and, behold, ye are this day as the stars of heaven for multitude."

adults.question: What qualification did Moses require of the judges he appointed?
adults.option_a: Wise men, and understanding, and known among their tribes
adults.option_b: Men of great wealth and property
adults.option_c: Men who had served in the tabernacle
adults.option_d: Firstborn sons of each tribe
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:13 — "Take you wise men, and understanding, and known among your tribes, and I will make them rulers over you."

adults.question: What principle of justice did Moses command the judges regarding persons?
adults.option_a: Ye shall not respect persons in judgment; hear the small as well as the great
adults.option_b: Show respect to elders above all others
adults.option_c: Favor the testimony of priests above laymen
adults.option_d: Defer all difficult cases to the Levites
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:17 — "Ye shall not respect persons in judgment; but ye shall hear the small as well as the great."

adults.question: How did Moses describe the wilderness journey from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea?
adults.option_a: A journey of eleven days
adults.option_b: A journey of forty years
adults.option_c: A journey of three months
adults.option_d: A journey of seven days
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:2 — "There are eleven days' journey from Horeb by the way of mount Seir unto Kadesh-barnea."

adults.question: What comparison did Moses use to describe how God carried Israel through the wilderness?
adults.option_a: As a man doth bear his son
adults.option_b: As a shepherd carries a lamb
adults.option_c: As an eagle carries her young on her wings
adults.option_d: As a mother carries her child
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:31 — "The LORD thy God bare thee, as a man doth bear his son, in all the way that ye went."

adults.question: Why was Moses told he would not lead Israel into the promised land?
adults.option_a: Because God was angry with him also on Israel's account
adults.option_b: Because he was too old
adults.option_c: Because he had worshipped idols
adults.option_d: Because he refused to go to Pharaoh
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:37 — "Also the LORD was angry with me for your sakes, saying, Thou also shalt not go in thither."

adults.question: After the failed attack on the Amorites, where did the Israelites return and weep before the LORD?
adults.option_a: At the foot of Mount Sinai
adults.option_b: In Kadesh
adults.option_c: At the tabernacle entrance
adults.option_d: Beside the Jordan River
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 1:45 — "And ye returned and wept before the LORD; but the LORD would not hearken to your voice."

kids.question: How many years did Israel wander in the wilderness?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:14 — "And the space in which we came from Kadesh-barnea...was thirty and eight years."

kids.question: Who was the king of Heshbon that Israel defeated?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:26 — "And I sent messengers out of the wilderness of Kedemoth unto Sihon king of Heshbon."

kids.question: God told Israel not to fight against the people of which land because He had given it to the sons of Lot?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:9 — "And the LORD said unto me, Distress not the Moabites, neither contend with them in battle: for I will not give thee of their land for a possession; because I have given Ar unto the children of Lot for a possession."

teens.question: God told Israel to pass through the border of Edom carefully. Why were they not to provoke the Edomites?
teens.option_a: Because God had given Mount Seir to Esau and they were brethren of Israel
teens.option_b: Because the Edomites were too powerful
teens.option_c: Because Israel had made a treaty with them
teens.option_d: Because the Edomites worshipped the LORD
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:4-5 — "Ye are to pass through the coast of your brethren...take ye good heed unto yourselves...for I will not give you of their land, no, not so much as a foot breadth; because I have given mount Seir unto Esau for a possession."

teens.question: What instruction did God give Israel concerning the Ammonites?
teens.option_a: Distress them not, nor meddle with them
teens.option_b: Destroy them completely
teens.option_c: Make a covenant of peace with them
teens.option_d: Tax them as tributaries
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:19 — "And when thou comest nigh over against the children of Ammon, distress them not, nor meddle with them: for I will not give thee of the land of the children of Ammon any possession."

teens.question: What offer of peace did Moses make to Sihon king of Heshbon?
teens.option_a: Let us pass through thy land; we will go along by the high way
teens.option_b: Tribute and taxes each year
teens.option_c: Allow Israel to settle in part of his kingdom
teens.option_d: Surrender and become Israel's servants
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:27 — "Let me pass through thy land: I will go along by the high way, I will neither turn unto the right hand nor to the left."

teens.question: Why did Sihon refuse to let Israel pass through his land?
teens.option_a: Because the LORD hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate
teens.option_b: Because Moses had offended him
teens.option_c: Because his army was large enough to defeat Israel
teens.option_d: Because he feared Israel would take his women and children
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:30 — "But Sihon king of Heshbon would not let us pass by him: for the LORD thy God hardened his spirit, and made his heart obstinate."

teens.question: What happened to all the cities of Sihon after Israel's victory?
teens.option_a: They were utterly destroyed — men, women, and children
teens.option_b: They were left standing for Israel to inhabit
teens.option_c: They were given to the Levites as an inheritance
teens.option_d: They paid tribute to Israel
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:34 — "And we took all his cities at that time, and utterly destroyed the men, and the women, and the little ones of every city, we left none to remain."

adults.question: What event marked the end of the generation of warriors who had been condemned to die in the wilderness?
adults.option_a: The crossing of the Red Sea
adults.option_b: When all the men of war from among the people had consumed and died
adults.option_c: The death of Aaron on Mount Hor
adults.option_d: The capture of Sihon's kingdom
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:14-15 — "Until all the generation of the men of war were wasted out from among the host...for indeed the hand of the LORD was against them, to destroy them from among the host."

adults.question: What were the original inhabitants of Moab called before the Moabites drove them out?
adults.option_a: Emims
adults.option_b: Rephaim
adults.option_c: Nephilim
adults.option_d: Anakim
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:10-11 — "The Emims dwelt therein in times past, a people great, and many, and tall, as the Anakims; which also were accounted giants, as the Anakims."

adults.question: What were the original inhabitants of Ammon called, who were displaced before the Ammonites settled there?
adults.option_a: Zamzummims
adults.option_b: Horim
adults.option_c: Emims
adults.option_d: Anakims
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:20 — "That also was accounted a land of giants: giants dwelt therein in old time; and the Ammonites call them Zamzummims."

adults.question: God told Israel they were to buy meat and water from the Edomites with silver. What reason did He give for this?
adults.option_a: Because the LORD had blessed Israel in all the works of their hands and He knew their walking through the great wilderness
adults.option_b: To show generosity to all nations
adults.option_c: To fulfil the covenant with Abraham
adults.option_d: Because Israel had not yet enough supplies
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:7 — "For the LORD thy God hath blessed thee in all the works of thy hand: he knoweth thy walking through this great wilderness."

adults.question: Where did Israel turn after going through the wilderness of Moab, before confronting Sihon?
adults.option_a: Beside the brook Zered
adults.option_b: Through the plains of Jericho
adults.option_c: Through the valley of Arnon
adults.option_d: Through the mountain of Seir
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:13 — "Now rise up, said I, and get you over the brook Zered. And we went over the brook Zered."

adults.question: What specific promise did God make to Israel when delivering Sihon into their hands?
adults.option_a: I will begin to put the dread of thee and the fear of thee upon the nations under the whole heaven
adults.option_b: I will send hornets before thee to drive out the nations
adults.option_c: I will make thee a terror to all kingdoms
adults.option_d: Mine angel shall go before thee
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:25 — "This day will I begin to put the dread of thee and the fear of thee upon the nations that are under the whole heaven."

adults.question: Which territory did Israel possess after defeating Sihon, according to chapter 2?
adults.option_a: All the land of the Anakims
adults.option_b: From Arnon unto Jabbok and from the wilderness unto the Jordan
adults.option_c: The whole of the land of Canaan
adults.option_d: The territory of Bashan only
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:36-37 — "From Aroer, which is by the brink of the river of Arnon...there was not one city too strong for us."

adults.question: The Horim were the original inhabitants of Seir whom Esau's descendants dispossessed. What parallel does this establish in the narrative?
adults.option_a: That all Gentile nations were given land by God
adults.option_b: That God had sovereignly given each people their territorial inheritance just as He was giving Canaan to Israel
adults.option_c: That the Horim were relatives of the Israelites
adults.option_d: That military conquest is always justified
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 2:12 — "As Israel did unto the land of his possession, which the LORD gave unto them" — paralleling Esau's dispossession of Horim with Israel's coming dispossession of Canaan.

kids.question: Who was the giant king of Bashan that Israel defeated?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:1 — "Then we turned, and went up the way to Bashan: and Og the king of Bashan came out against us."

kids.question: Moses was not allowed to cross the Jordan River. Who would lead Israel instead?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:28 — "But charge Joshua, and encourage him, and strengthen him: for he shall go over before this people."

kids.question: How large was the iron bed of King Og of Bashan?
kids.option_b: Nine cubits long and four wide
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:11 — "His bedstead was a bedstead of iron...nine cubits was the length thereof, and four cubits the breadth of it."

teens.question: What did God say to Moses before the battle against Og to give him courage?
teens.option_a: Fear him not, for I have delivered him into thy hand
teens.option_b: I will send fire from heaven to destroy him
teens.option_c: The LORD your God fights for you
teens.option_d: Take courage, for the LORD is with thee
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:2 — "And the LORD said unto me, Fear him not: for I will deliver him, and all his people, and his land, into thy hand."

teens.question: How many cities did Israel take in the region of Argob in Bashan?
teens.option_a: Threescore
teens.option_b: Twenty
teens.option_c: Twelve
teens.option_d: Forty
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:4 — "And we took all his cities at that time, there was not a city which we took not from them, threescore cities."

teens.question: Which tribes received the territory of Sihon and Og east of the Jordan?
teens.option_a: Reuben, Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh
teens.option_b: Judah, Simeon, and Levi
teens.option_c: Ephraim, Benjamin, and Dan
teens.option_d: Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:12-13 — "And this land...I gave unto the Reubenites and to the Gadites. And the rest of Gilead, and all Bashan...gave I unto the half tribe of Manasseh."

teens.question: What condition did Moses place on the warriors of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh before they could inherit their land east of Jordan?
teens.option_a: They must first build cities of refuge
teens.option_b: They must pass armed before their brethren into Canaan until the LORD gave rest to all Israel
teens.option_c: They must pay tribute for ten years
teens.option_d: They must study the law for one year
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:18 — "I commanded you at that time, saying...ye shall pass over armed before your brethren the children of Israel."

teens.question: What was Moses' earnest prayer to God at the end of chapter 3?
teens.option_a: That God would let him cross over and see the good land beyond Jordan
teens.option_b: That the Israelites would obey the law
teens.option_c: That Joshua would be made strong
teens.option_d: That Israel would destroy all the Canaanites
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:25 — "I pray thee, let me go over, and see the good land that is beyond Jordan, that goodly mountain, and Lebanon."

adults.question: What comparison does Moses draw between the victory over Og and the earlier victory over Sihon?
adults.option_a: As I did unto Sihon king of Heshbon, so the LORD our God delivered Og also unto us
adults.option_b: The victory over Og was greater because he was a giant
adults.option_c: The victory over Og required less fighting because the cities were smaller
adults.option_d: Og surrendered willingly while Sihon fought to the death
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:2 — The LORD said "Fear him not" and delivered him as He did Sihon, establishing a pattern of divine conquest.

adults.question: What distinguished the cities of the plain from the fenced cities taken from Og?
adults.option_a: The fenced cities had high walls, gates, and bars; the unwalled country villages were many
adults.option_b: The plain cities were given to the priests
adults.option_c: The plain cities were burned but the fenced cities kept intact
adults.option_d: The fenced cities were given to Levites and the plain cities to warriors
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:5 — "All these cities were fenced with high walls, gates, and bars; beside unwalled towns a great many."

adults.question: What note does Moses include about the Rephaim to explain Og's gigantic size?
adults.option_a: Og was the last of the remnant of giants
adults.option_b: Og was the last of the Anakim
adults.option_c: Og was descended from Nephilim through Ham
adults.option_d: Og was born of a human mother and angelic father
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:11 — "For only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of giants; behold, his bedstead was a bedstead of iron."

adults.question: What was the name of the Amorite territory that Jair of Manasseh captured and renamed?
adults.option_a: He called it Bashan-havoth
adults.option_b: He called it Havoth-jair after himself
adults.option_c: He called it Gilead
adults.option_d: He called it Argob
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:14 — "Jair the son of Manasseh took all the country of Argob unto the coasts of Geshuri and Maachathi; and called them after his own name, Bashan-havoth-jair."

adults.question: What mountain was Moses permitted to climb to view the promised land he could not enter?
adults.option_a: The top of Pisgah
adults.option_b: Mount Sinai
adults.option_c: Mount Hor
adults.option_d: Mount Nebo
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:27 — "Get thee up into the top of Pisgah, and lift up thine eyes westward, and northward, and southward, and eastward, and behold it with thine eyes."

adults.question: How did God respond to Moses' prayer to cross the Jordan?
adults.option_a: The LORD was wroth and told Moses to speak no more of that matter
adults.option_b: God promised to reconsider
adults.option_c: God told Moses to wait until Joshua led Israel across
adults.option_d: God was silent and gave no answer
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:26 — "But the LORD was wroth with me for your sakes, and would not hear me: and the LORD said unto me, Let it suffice thee; speak no more unto me of this matter."

adults.question: What was the primary responsibility Moses gave Joshua regarding the conquest?
adults.option_a: To go over this Jordan — thou shalt cause them to inherit the land
adults.option_b: To administer the law and judge the people
adults.option_c: To build the tabernacle in Canaan
adults.option_d: To lead worship after crossing the Jordan
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:28 — "Charge Joshua, and encourage him, and strengthen him: for he shall go over before this people, and he shall cause them to inherit the land."

adults.question: Beth-peor is mentioned as the location opposite which Moses spoke to Israel. What significance does this place have later in the Pentateuch?
adults.option_a: It was where Israel sinned with Baal-peor in Numbers 25
adults.option_b: It was the place of Moses' death and burial
adults.option_c: It was the first city conquered in Canaan
adults.option_d: It was the location of the tabernacle
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 3:29 — "So we abode in the valley over against Beth-peor" — the same valley where Israel had earlier committed idolatry at Baal-peor (Numbers 25).

kids.question: Moses told Israel not to make statues or pictures of what kind of things to worship?
kids.option_b: Any figure from heaven, earth, or water
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:16-18 — Israel was commanded not to make any graven image of any figure, the likeness of male or female, beast, bird, creeping thing, or fish.

kids.question: Moses set apart three special cities east of the Jordan River for what purpose?
kids.option_b: For people who accidentally killed someone to flee to for safety
kids.option_c: For the tribes to worship in
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:41-42 — "Then Moses severed three cities on this side Jordan...that the slayer might flee thither, which should kill his neighbour unawares."

kids.question: What did Moses say God was like that made Him different from any idol?
kids.option_a: God was like a consuming fire
kids.option_b: God was like a powerful king
kids.option_c: God was like a bright light
kids.option_d: God was like a mighty warrior
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:24 — "For the LORD thy God is a consuming fire, even a jealous God."

teens.question: Why did Moses warn Israel not to add to or subtract from God's commandments?
teens.option_a: So that they might keep them and be seen as wise and understanding in the eyes of the nations
teens.option_b: Because Moses had written them perfectly
teens.option_c: Because the priests would punish them
teens.option_d: Because only priests were allowed to interpret the law
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:2,6 — "Ye shall not add unto the word...that ye might keep the commandments...for this is your wisdom and your understanding in the sight of the nations."

teens.question: What happened at Baal-peor that Moses used as a warning to Israel?
teens.option_a: The tabernacle was destroyed by fire
teens.option_b: All who followed Baal-peor were destroyed; those who held fast to the LORD all lived
teens.option_c: Israel was defeated in battle
teens.option_d: A plague killed the Levites
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:3-4 — "Your eyes have seen what the LORD did because of Baal-peor: for all the men that followed Baal-peor, the LORD thy God hath destroyed them...but ye that did cleave unto the LORD your God are alive every one of you."

teens.question: What unique privilege did Moses say no other nation had compared to Israel?
teens.option_a: A great nation with God so nigh unto them as the LORD was to Israel
teens.option_b: A land flowing with milk and honey
teens.option_c: The largest army of all peoples
teens.option_d: A king who was chosen by God
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:7 — "What nation is there so great, who hath God so nigh unto them, as the LORD our God is in all things that we call upon him for?"

teens.question: What did Moses predict would happen if Israel made idols and did evil in the land?
teens.option_a: They would be scattered among the nations and serve gods of wood and stone
teens.option_b: They would lose one battle but recover
teens.option_c: Their crops would fail for seven years
teens.option_d: Their firstborn would be taken
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:27-28 — "The LORD shall scatter you among the nations...and there ye shall serve gods, the work of men's hands, wood and stone."

teens.question: What hope did Moses give Israel even if they were scattered among the nations for idolatry?
teens.option_a: If they sought the LORD with all their heart they would find Him and He would not forsake them
teens.option_b: God would raise up a new prophet like Moses
teens.option_c: A new generation would inherit the land
teens.option_d: God would destroy all their enemies
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:29-31 — "But if from thence thou shalt seek the LORD thy God, thou shalt find him, if thou seek him with all thy heart...he will not forsake thee."

adults.question: What theological argument does Moses make from the Horeb theophany to forbid image worship?
adults.option_a: God is too holy to be represented
adults.option_b: Ye saw no manner of similitude on the day the LORD spake out of the fire — therefore take heed lest ye make a graven image
adults.option_c: God commanded that no artist could depict divine things
adults.option_d: The Ten Commandments were written on stone to show God's permanence
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:12,15 — "Ye heard the voice of the words, but saw no similitude...Take ye therefore good heed unto yourselves; for ye saw no manner of similitude."

adults.question: Moses commands Israel to teach the covenant statutes diligently to their children. What specific domestic context does he describe?
adults.option_a: When thou sittest in thine house, walkest by the way, liest down, and risest up
adults.option_b: In the synagogue every Sabbath
adults.option_c: During the annual feasts at the tabernacle
adults.option_d: When the priests visit each family
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4 connects to chapter 6:7 where Moses says to teach them diligently...when thou sittest in thine house, and when thou walkest by the way.

adults.question: What is the unique claim Moses makes about Israel's law compared to the statutes of other nations?
adults.option_a: What nation is there so great, that hath statutes and judgments so righteous as all this law?
adults.option_b: Israel's law was written by God Himself on stone
adults.option_c: Israel's law covers more subjects than any other code
adults.option_d: Only Israel's law includes ceremonial, civil, and moral categories
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:8 — "And what nation is there so great, that hath statutes and judgments so righteous as all this law, which I set before you this day?"

adults.question: What are the three cities of refuge Moses designated east of Jordan in chapter 4?
adults.option_a: Bezer, Ramoth in Gilead, and Golan in Bashan
adults.option_b: Hebron, Shechem, and Kedesh
adults.option_c: Peniel, Mahanaim, and Succoth
adults.option_d: Aroer, Dibon, and Medeba
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:43 — "Namely, Bezer in the wilderness...Ramoth in Gilead...and Golan in Bashan."

adults.question: How does Moses describe the covenant God made at Horeb in terms of its intended recipients?
adults.option_a: Not with our fathers, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day
adults.option_b: God made it with the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
adults.option_c: God made it with all mankind through Noah
adults.option_d: God made it with Moses alone as representative
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:3 (anticipated in ch 4) — "The LORD made not this covenant with our fathers, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day."

adults.question: What comprehensive statement about the LORD's uniqueness does Moses make in Deuteronomy 4:35?
adults.option_a: The LORD he is God; there is none else beside him
adults.option_b: The LORD is the strongest of all gods
adults.option_c: The LORD is the creator of heaven and earth alone
adults.option_d: The LORD is God of Israel only
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:35 — "Unto thee it was shewed, that thou mightest know that the LORD he is God; there is none else beside him."

adults.question: For what purpose did Moses say God brought Israel out of Egypt with signs, wonders, and a mighty hand?
adults.option_a: To bring them in, to give them the land, and to drive out nations greater and mightier than they
adults.option_b: To give them the most fertile land on earth
adults.option_c: To show all nations that Israel was His favourite
adults.option_d: To establish a priestly kingdom for all peoples
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:37-38 — "He brought thee out in his sight with his mighty power out of Egypt; to drive out nations from before thee greater and mightier than thou art."

adults.question: What eschatological dimension does Moses include in Deuteronomy 4 regarding Israel's future repentance?
adults.option_a: In the latter days Israel would turn to the LORD and He would not forget the covenant sworn to the fathers
adults.option_b: God would send a new Moses to restore them
adults.option_c: Israel would build a new temple greater than Solomon's
adults.option_d: A king like David would gather the scattered tribes
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 4:30-31 — "When thou art in tribulation, and all these things are come upon thee, even in the latter days, if thou turn to the LORD thy God...he will not forget the covenant of thy fathers."

kids.question: Moses repeated God's special rules in chapter 5. What are these rules called?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:6-21 — Moses restated the Ten Commandments given at Horeb.

kids.question: On which day did God command Israel to rest and do no work?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:14 — "The seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work."

kids.question: What did God write the Ten Commandments on when He first gave them at the mountain?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:22 — "These words the LORD spake...and he wrote them in two tables of stone."

teens.question: How does the Sabbath commandment in Deuteronomy 5 differ from the version in Exodus 20 in its reason for rest?
teens.option_a: Deuteronomy adds servants to those who must rest
teens.option_b: Deuteronomy grounds the Sabbath in the Exodus from Egypt rather than creation
teens.option_c: Deuteronomy makes the Sabbath stricter by forbidding travel
teens.option_d: Deuteronomy says foreigners are excluded from Sabbath rest
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:15 — "Remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt...therefore the LORD thy God commanded thee to keep the sabbath day" — grounding it in redemption, not creation.

teens.question: What was the people's reaction when they heard God's voice from the fire at Horeb?
teens.option_a: They fell on their faces and praised God
teens.option_b: They begged Moses to be their mediator so they would not die
teens.option_c: They built an altar and sacrificed
teens.option_d: They ran into the cloud and sought God's face
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:25 — "Now therefore why should we die? for this great fire will consume us...if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we shall die."

teens.question: What did God say about Israel's desire to have a human mediator instead of hearing His voice directly?
teens.option_a: He was displeased that they feared Him
teens.option_b: He said they had done well and wished their hearts would always fear Him and keep His commandments
teens.option_c: He told them they were unworthy of His presence
teens.option_d: He appointed Moses as permanent high priest
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:28-29 — "They have well said all that they have spoken. O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always."

teens.question: What commandment promises long life for those who keep it?
teens.option_a: Honour thy father and thy mother
teens.option_b: Keep the Sabbath
teens.option_c: Thou shalt not kill
teens.option_d: Thou shalt have no other gods
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:16 — "Honour thy father and thy mother...that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee, in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee."

teens.question: What role did God assign Moses after the people asked him to be their mediator?
teens.option_a: To build the tabernacle
teens.option_b: To stand between God and Israel, hear all God says, and teach them
teens.option_c: To write the laws on stone again
teens.option_d: To lead the army into battle
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:31 — "But as for thee, stand thou here by me, and I will speak unto thee all the commandments...and thou shalt teach them."

adults.question: What exact claim does Moses make about the Ten Commandments and the Sinai fire in verse 22?
adults.option_a: These words the LORD spake unto all your assembly out of the midst of the fire...with a great voice and he added no more, and wrote them in two tables
adults.option_b: God commanded them in an audible voice from a cloud
adults.option_c: Moses received them alone on the mountain top
adults.option_d: The elders of Israel also heard these words directly
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:22 — "These words the LORD spake unto all your assembly...with a great voice: and he added no more. And he wrote them in two tables of stone."

adults.question: What theological phrase introduces the Ten Commandments in Deuteronomy 5:6?
adults.option_a: I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage
adults.option_b: I am the LORD thy God, who made heaven and earth
adults.option_c: I am the LORD, the God of thy fathers
adults.option_d: I am the LORD thy God, the Holy One of Israel
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:6 — "I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage."

adults.question: The commandment against coveting in Deuteronomy 5 lists one item first that differs from the Exodus 20 version. What is it?
adults.option_a: In Deuteronomy, the neighbour's wife is listed before his house
adults.option_b: In Deuteronomy, the neighbour's field is added after his house
adults.option_c: In Deuteronomy, the neighbour's servant is listed first
adults.option_d: In Deuteronomy, the list is shorter and ends with wife
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:21 — "Neither shalt thou desire thy neighbour's wife, neither shalt thou covet thy neighbour's house, his field..." — wife is listed first, then house and field.

adults.question: What statement does Moses make about the audience in chapter 5 to emphasize the covenant's immediacy?
adults.option_a: The LORD made this covenant not with our fathers but with us who are all here alive this day
adults.option_b: Their fathers received this covenant at Sinai
adults.option_c: The covenant is for future generations only
adults.option_d: The covenant was made once and cannot be renewed
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:3 — "The LORD made not this covenant with our fathers, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day."

adults.question: What physical phenomenon accompanied God's speaking at Horeb, according to Deuteronomy 5?
adults.option_a: Fire, cloud, thick darkness, and a great voice
adults.option_b: Mighty earthquakes and floods
adults.option_c: An earthquake and the mountain splitting
adults.option_d: Lightning and the sound of many waters
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:22-23 — "Out of the midst of the fire, of the cloud, and of the thick darkness, with a great voice."

adults.question: What were Moses' responsibilities regarding the commandments that he received beyond the Ten?
adults.option_a: He was to add his own interpretations
adults.option_b: He was to teach them statutes and judgments which they should do in the land they were going to possess
adults.option_c: He was to write them on the doorposts of the tabernacle
adults.option_d: He was to deliver them to the priests only
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:31 — "I will speak unto thee all the commandments, and the statutes, and the judgments, which thou shalt teach them."

adults.question: How does God describe His attitude toward those who love Him and keep His commandments in the second commandment's preamble?
adults.option_a: Shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me and keep my commandments
adults.option_b: He is patient with them
adults.option_c: He will bless them above all peoples
adults.option_d: He will give them wisdom and long life
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:10 — "And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me and keep my commandments."

adults.question: God's statement "I the LORD thy God am a jealous God" appears in which commandment context?
adults.option_a: The fourth commandment concerning the Sabbath
adults.option_b: The second commandment forbidding graven images
adults.option_c: The first commandment concerning other gods
adults.option_d: The fifth commandment concerning parents
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 5:9 — "Thou shalt not bow down thyself unto them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God."

kids.question: What is the famous verse that begins "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD"?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:4 — "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD." This verse is called the Shema.

kids.question: God told Israel to write His commandments on two special places in their homes. Where were they?
kids.option_a: On the walls and the floor
kids.option_d: On the ceiling and the door
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:9 — "And thou shalt write them upon the posts of thy house, and on thy gates."

kids.question: How much did God say Israel should love the LORD?
kids.option_a: With all thine heart, soul, and might
kids.option_b: A little bit, when they have time
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:5 — "And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might."

teens.question: What is the Hebrew term for the prayer that begins "Hear, O Israel" in Deuteronomy 6:4-9?
teens.option_a: The Shema
teens.option_b: The Hallel
teens.option_c: The Amidah
teens.option_d: The Kiddush
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:4 — "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD" — this passage is known as the Shema, from the Hebrew word for "hear."

teens.question: Moses warned Israel about forgetting God when they prospered in the land. What specific temptation did he name?
teens.option_a: Fighting against neighbouring nations
teens.option_b: Eating and being full, building houses, and saying in thine heart My power hath gotten me this wealth
teens.option_c: Intermarrying with Canaanites
teens.option_d: Refusing to bring tithes to the priests
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:11-12 — "When thou shalt have eaten and be full...then beware lest thou forget the LORD."

teens.question: What instruction did Moses give for parents when their children ask the meaning of the commandments?
teens.option_a: Say unto them, We were Pharaoh's bondmen in Egypt and the LORD brought us out
teens.option_b: Send them to a priest for instruction
teens.option_c: Tell them to read the law themselves
teens.option_d: Bring them to the tabernacle for teaching
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:20-21 — "And when thy son asketh thee in time to come...thou shalt say unto thy son, We were Pharaoh's bondmen in Egypt."

teens.question: What warning did Moses give about testing the LORD their God?
teens.option_a: Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah
teens.option_b: They should test Him only in cases of great need
teens.option_c: Testing God was reserved for priests only
teens.option_d: Testing was permitted in times of war
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:16 — "Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah."

teens.question: How did Moses describe the purpose of the commandments regarding Israel's righteousness before God?
teens.option_a: It shall be our righteousness if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD
teens.option_b: The commandments made Israel greater than all nations
teens.option_c: Keeping commandments earned Israel military victories
teens.option_d: The commandments were Israel's gift back to God
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:25 — "And it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD our God, as he hath commanded us."

adults.question: What threefold purpose does Moses give for the commandments at the opening of chapter 6?
adults.option_a: That they might fear the LORD, keep His statutes, and that their days may be prolonged
adults.option_b: To make Israel wealthy, wise, and warlike
adults.option_c: To set Israel apart from all nations permanently
adults.option_d: To prepare Israel for priestly service
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:2 — "That thou mightest fear the LORD thy God, to keep all his statutes and his commandments...that thy days may be prolonged."

adults.question: The command to bind God's words "as a sign upon thine hand" and as "frontlets between thine eyes" in Deuteronomy 6:8 is the basis for which Jewish practice?
adults.option_a: Tefillin (phylacteries)
adults.option_b: Mezuzot on doorposts
adults.option_c: Tzitzit on garments
adults.option_d: Wearing the priestly breastplate
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:8 — "And thou shalt bind them for a sign upon thine hand, and they shall be as frontlets between thine eyes" — the basis of tefillin.

adults.question: In what context does Jesus quote Deuteronomy 6:5 in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: When healing a blind man
adults.option_b: When answering which is the great commandment of the law
adults.option_c: In the Sermon on the Mount
adults.option_d: When cleansing the temple
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:5 — "Thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart" — Jesus quotes this as the greatest commandment in Matthew 22:37.

adults.question: What diligent teaching practice does Moses prescribe for transmitting the commandments to children?
adults.option_a: Thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house, and when thou walkest by the way, and when thou liest down, and when thou risest up
adults.option_b: Weekly recitation in the assembly
adults.option_c: Annual reading at the Passover feast
adults.option_d: Having the Levites instruct each family
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:7 — "And thou shalt teach them diligently unto thy children, and shalt talk of them when thou sittest in thine house."

adults.question: What specific danger does Moses highlight about the prosperity Israel will find in Canaan's already-built cities?
adults.option_a: Lest thou forget the LORD which brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage
adults.option_b: Israel might become too proud to fight
adults.option_c: The Canaanites might reclaim their houses
adults.option_d: Israel might not know how to farm the land
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:10-12 — "When the LORD thy God shall have brought thee into the land...beware lest thou forget the LORD."

adults.question: God promises to drive out which nations before Israel according to chapter 6?
adults.option_a: Seven nations: the Hittite, Girgashite, Amorite, Canaanite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite
adults.option_b: Three nations: the Canaanites, Philistines, and Hittites
adults.option_c: All twelve tribes remaining in the land
adults.option_d: Five nations: Midian, Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Canaan
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:1 (immediately following ch 6) enumerates "seven nations greater and mightier than thou"; in ch 6 Moses anticipates this by warning against their gods.

adults.question: What does Moses command Israel to do with the commandments they are to love and obey?
adults.option_a: These words shall be in thine heart — to teach, bind, write on posts and gates
adults.option_b: Engrave them only on the tabernacle furnishings
adults.option_c: Recite them only on the Sabbath
adults.option_d: Keep them in the ark of the covenant
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:6-9 — "These words...shall be in thine heart...teach them...talk of them...bind them...write them upon the posts."

adults.question: What does Moses say Israel's diligent keeping of God's commandments will produce as a final outcome?
adults.option_a: It will be righteousness unto them if they observe to do all these commandments
adults.option_b: They will become the wealthiest nation on earth
adults.option_c: Their army will be unconquerable
adults.option_d: They will receive a new covenant with greater promises
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 6:25 — "And it shall be our righteousness, if we observe to do all these commandments before the LORD our God."

kids.question: God commanded Israel not to marry the people of which lands they were entering?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:1-3 — "When the LORD thy God shall bring thee into the land...thou shalt make no marriages with them."

kids.question: Why did God choose Israel to be His special people?
kids.option_a: Because they were the biggest nation
kids.option_b: Not because they were many but because God loved them and kept His oath to the fathers
kids.option_c: Because they were the cleverest people
kids.option_d: Because they had the best soldiers
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:6-8 — "The LORD did not set his love upon you, nor choose you, because ye were more in number...but because the LORD loved you."

kids.question: What were Israel supposed to destroy when they conquered the Canaanite nations?
kids.option_a: Their altars, images, groves, and graven images
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:5 — "But thus shall ye deal with them; ye shall destroy their altars, and break down their images, and cut down their groves, and burn their graven images."

teens.question: What does Moses mean by calling Israel "an holy people" and "a peculiar people" unto the LORD?
teens.option_a: Israel was strange compared to other nations
teens.option_b: Israel was set apart and specially treasured as God's own possession above all people on earth
teens.option_c: Israel had unusual customs that made them different
teens.option_d: Israel was forbidden from all contact with foreigners
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:6 — "For thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God: the LORD thy God hath chosen thee to be a special people unto himself, above all people that are upon the face of the earth."

teens.question: How did God describe His faithfulness to those who love Him and keep His commandments?
teens.option_a: He would keep covenant and mercy for a thousand generations
teens.option_b: He would give them victory in every battle
teens.option_c: He would multiply their cattle and harvests
teens.option_d: He would let them live forever
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:9 — "Know therefore that the LORD thy God, he is God, the faithful God, which keepeth covenant and mercy with them that love him and keep his commandments to a thousand generations."

teens.question: What would happen to those who hated God according to verse 10?
teens.option_a: He would repay them that hate him to their face to destroy them
teens.option_b: They would be enslaved
teens.option_c: They would be banished from the land
teens.option_d: They would lose their prosperity
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:10 — "And repayeth them that hate him to their face, to destroy them: he will not be slack to him that hateth him."

teens.question: What strategy did God say He would use against the Canaanites — not all at once but little by little, and why?
teens.option_a: Lest the beast of the field increase upon thee
teens.option_b: Because Israel's army was still small
teens.option_c: Because the cities needed to be inspected first
teens.option_d: Because Israel needed time to learn farming
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:22 — "The LORD thy God will put out those nations before thee by little and little: thou mayest not consume them at once, lest the beasts of the field increase upon thee."

teens.question: What did Moses tell Israel to do with the silver and gold on the Canaanite idols?
teens.option_a: Not desire it nor take it, for it was an abomination
teens.option_b: Melt it down for the tabernacle
teens.option_c: Give it to the Levites
teens.option_d: Bury it in the ground
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:25 — "Thou shalt not desire the silver or gold that is on them, nor take it unto thee, lest thou be snared therein: for it is an abomination to the LORD thy God."

adults.question: What is the Hebrew concept behind the command to "utterly destroy" the Canaanite nations, and what term is used in later scholarship for this practice?
adults.option_a: The concept of cherem — total consecration/destruction to the LORD
adults.option_b: Complete annihilation called jihad
adults.option_c: A military strategy called scorched earth
adults.option_d: A purification ritual called kiddush
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:2 — "And when the LORD thy God shall deliver them before thee; thou shalt smite them, and utterly destroy them" — the Hebrew cherem implies total devotion to God through destruction.

adults.question: What specific covenantal act does Moses forbid Israel from making with the seven nations, and what is the explicitly stated reason?
adults.option_a: No trade agreements, lest they become economically dependent
adults.option_b: No covenants or showing mercy, for they would turn Israel from following the LORD to serve other gods
adults.option_c: No intermarriage only, to preserve the genetic line
adults.option_d: No military alliances, because God would fight for Israel alone
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:2-4 — "Thou shalt make no covenant with them...neither shalt thou make marriages with them...for they will turn away thy son from following me."

adults.question: How does Moses describe the blessing that will follow obedience in the land in verses 13-15?
adults.option_a: The LORD will love, bless, and multiply thee — blessing fruit of womb, land, grain, wine, oil, cattle — and remove all sickness
adults.option_b: Israel will rule all nations as a superpower
adults.option_c: Israel will receive tribute from all surrounding kingdoms
adults.option_d: The land will yield harvests a hundredfold
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:13-15 — "He will love thee, and bless thee, and multiply thee...he will also bless the fruit of thy womb...and the LORD will take away from thee all sickness."

adults.question: What theological reason does Moses give for Israel not fearing the more numerous Canaanite nations?
adults.option_a: Remember what the LORD did unto Pharaoh and unto Egypt — the same mighty hand and outstretched arm would deliver
adults.option_b: Israel had better weapons
adults.option_c: Angels would precede Israel into battle
adults.option_d: The Canaanites' gods could not help them
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:18-19 — "Thou shalt not be afraid of them: but shalt well remember what the LORD thy God did unto Pharaoh, and unto all Egypt."

adults.question: Moses promises God will send the hornet before Israel. What is the significance of this sign?
adults.option_a: It represents God's sting of judgment on the wicked
adults.option_b: God would use natural means as well as supernatural to drive out the nations before Israel arrived
adults.option_c: The hornet was the symbol of Egypt and would terrorize the Canaanites
adults.option_d: It was a literal plague of insects that weakened the Canaanites
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:20 — "Moreover the LORD thy God will send the hornet among them, until they that are left, and hide themselves from thee, be destroyed."

adults.question: What promise does God make about His own power in relation to the nations that appear greater and mightier than Israel?
adults.option_a: The LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible
adults.option_b: God will weaken them with disease before battle
adults.option_c: God will confuse the nations with strange languages
adults.option_d: God will send fire from heaven on their cities
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:21 — "Thou shalt not be affrighted at them: for the LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible."

adults.question: The covenant faithfulness of God in Deuteronomy 7 spans "a thousand generations." What does this communicate about the nature of God's covenant love?
adults.option_a: The covenant will expire after a thousand generations
adults.option_b: Divine hesed (covenant mercy/love) is immeasurably greater in duration than human generations
adults.option_c: God calculated exactly how many generations Israel would last
adults.option_d: The covenant was mainly for ancient Israelites
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:9 — "keepeth covenant and mercy with them that love him...to a thousand generations" — expressing infinite divine faithfulness.

adults.question: How does Moses describe the Canaanite idols in terms of their ultimate fate and the fate of anyone who is attracted to them?
adults.option_a: They are valueless curiosities to be ignored
adults.option_b: They are an abomination and a snare, and the detestable thing must be utterly abhorred and abominated
adults.option_c: They are tempting but powerless
adults.option_d: They may be kept as trophies of victory
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 7:25-26 — "Thou shalt not bring an abomination into thine house...but thou shalt utterly detest it, and thou shalt utterly abhor it; for it is a cursed thing."

kids.question: What special food did God give Israel to eat in the wilderness that they had never seen before?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:3 — "He...suffered thee to hunger, and fed thee with manna, which thou knewest not, neither did thy fathers know."

kids.question: Moses said man does not live by bread alone but by what?
kids.option_a: Every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:3 — "Man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD doth man live."

kids.question: What miracle happened to Israel's clothes and feet during forty years in the wilderness?
kids.option_b: Their clothes did not wear out and their feet did not swell
kids.option_c: God gave them shoes made of iron
kids.option_d: Their sandals grew back when broken
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:4 — "Thy raiment waxed not old upon thee, neither did thy foot swell, these forty years."

teens.question: What was the stated purpose of God humbling Israel and letting them hunger in the wilderness?
teens.option_a: To prove what was in their heart, whether they would keep his commandments or not
teens.option_b: To punish them for the golden calf
teens.option_c: To show the surrounding nations God's power
teens.option_d: To teach them to depend on priests for food
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:2 — "To humble thee, and to prove thee, to know what was in thine heart, whether thou wouldest keep his commandments, or no."

teens.question: Moses described the promised land as "a good land." What bodies of water did he say it had?
teens.option_a: Brooks of water, of fountains and depths that spring out of valleys and hills
teens.option_b: Great rivers like the Nile and Euphrates
teens.option_c: A great sea on every side
teens.option_d: Vast underground lakes
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:7 — "A land of brooks of water, of fountains and depths that spring out of valleys and hills."

teens.question: What were Israel supposed to do after eating and being satisfied in the good land?
teens.option_a: Bless the LORD their God for the good land he had given them
teens.option_b: Build temples on the hilltops
teens.option_c: Offer the firstfruits to Moses
teens.option_d: Plant more of the crops they had harvested
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:10 — "When thou hast eaten and art full, then thou shalt bless the LORD thy God for the good land which he hath given thee."

teens.question: What dangerous attitude did Moses warn Israel against when they became prosperous and wealthy?
teens.option_a: Saying in their heart, My power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth
teens.option_b: Forgetting the Sabbath
teens.option_c: Refusing to go to war
teens.option_d: Building too large a city
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:17 — "And thou say in thine heart, My power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth."

teens.question: What consequence did Moses say would come if Israel forgot the LORD and followed other gods?
teens.option_a: Ye shall surely perish, as the nations whom the LORD destroyeth before your face
teens.option_b: They would lose only their wealth
teens.option_c: Their crops would fail for one season
teens.option_d: They would lose only their military power
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:19-20 — "If thou do at all forget the LORD thy God...ye shall surely perish, as the nations which the LORD destroyeth before your face."

adults.question: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 in response to which temptation in the wilderness?
adults.option_a: The temptation to cast himself from the temple
adults.option_b: The temptation to turn stones into bread
adults.option_c: The temptation to bow to Satan
adults.option_d: The temptation to call angels for help
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:3 — "Man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD" — quoted in Matthew 4:4.

adults.question: What relationship does Moses draw between the fatherly discipline of a man and God's discipline of Israel?
adults.option_a: As a man chasteneth his son, so the LORD thy God chasteneth thee
adults.option_b: As a teacher disciplines a student for knowledge
adults.option_c: As a king disciplines a subject for rebellion
adults.option_d: As a shepherd corrects a wayward sheep
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:5 — "Thou shalt also consider in thine heart, that, as a man chasteneth his son, so the LORD thy God chasteneth thee."

adults.question: The "good land" described in Deuteronomy 8 includes what mineral resources, demonstrating its material abundance?
adults.option_a: A land whose stones are iron, and out of whose hills thou mayest dig brass
adults.option_b: Gold and silver in great quantity
adults.option_c: Precious gems in the rivers and hills
adults.option_d: Salt mines and sulfur springs
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:9 — "A land whose stones are iron, and out of whose hills thou mayest dig brass."

adults.question: What seven agricultural products describe the fertility of the promised land in Deuteronomy 8:8?
adults.option_a: Wheat, barley, vines, fig trees, pomegranates, olive trees, and honey
adults.option_b: Wheat, barley, figs, olives, dates, flax, and flint
adults.option_c: Wheat, millet, corn, grapes, almonds, walnuts, and dates
adults.option_d: Barley, rye, spelt, grapes, olives, flax, and leeks
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:8 — "A land of wheat, and barley, and vines, and fig trees, and pomegranates; a land of oil olive, and honey."

adults.question: What specific cognitive and spiritual danger does Moses associate with material prosperity in chapter 8?
adults.option_a: Lest when thou hast eaten and art full...thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD
adults.option_b: Pride in personal achievement that leads to forgetting divine grace
adults.option_c: Addiction to luxury that prevents prayer
adults.option_d: Satisfying physical needs at the expense of spiritual disciplines
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:12-14 — "Lest when thou hast eaten and art full...thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD thy God."

adults.question: What rhetorical correction does Moses offer to the temptation toward self-sufficiency in verse 17?
adults.option_a: But thou shalt remember the LORD thy God: for it is he that giveth thee power to get wealth
adults.option_b: That Israel's wealth was borrowed from God
adults.option_c: That God could take the wealth away at any moment
adults.option_d: That Moses was the true source of Israel's blessings
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:18 — "But thou shalt remember the LORD thy God: for it is he that giveth thee power to get wealth."

adults.question: How does Moses describe the wilderness in chapter 8 to contrast God's miraculous provision with natural scarcity?
adults.option_a: That great and terrible wilderness, wherein were fiery serpents, and scorpions, and drought
adults.option_b: A dry and thirsty land with no water
adults.option_c: A land of thorns and thistles and constant danger
adults.option_d: A vast empty desert with no food or shelter
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:15 — "Who led thee through that great and terrible wilderness, wherein were fiery serpents, and scorpions, and drought."

adults.question: What covenantal purpose does God's provision of wealth connect to, according to verse 18's conclusion?
adults.option_a: That he may establish his covenant which he sware unto thy fathers
adults.option_b: To make Israel an example to Gentile nations
adults.option_c: To prove Israel is more righteous than the nations
adults.option_d: To prepare Israel for the coming of a messianic king
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 8:18 — "It is he that giveth thee power to get wealth, that he may establish his covenant which he sware unto thy fathers."

kids.question: What did Israel do with a golden animal while Moses was on the mountain with God?
kids.option_d: They melted gold into a serpent
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:16 — "And I looked, and, behold, ye had sinned against the LORD your God, and had made you a molten calf."

kids.question: How many days and nights did Moses stay on the mountain without eating or drinking?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:9 — "I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water."

kids.question: Moses told Israel they were not getting the promised land because they were better than the Canaanites. Was this true?
kids.option_b: No, it was because of the Canaanites' wickedness and God's promise to the fathers
kids.option_c: Yes, because Israel obeyed all of God's laws
kids.option_d: No, Moses said they were too small a nation
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:4-5 — "Speak not thou in thine heart...Not for thy righteousness...but for the wickedness of these nations...and that he may perform the word which the LORD sware unto thy fathers."

teens.question: What did Moses say about Israel's character at the very beginning of chapter 9 to set up his warning?
teens.option_a: Thou art to pass over Jordan to possess nations greater than thee, whose cities are great and walled up to heaven
teens.option_b: Thou art a holy and obedient people
teens.option_c: Thou art a strong and mighty nation
teens.option_d: Thou art beloved among all peoples on earth
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:1 — "Hear, O Israel: Thou art to pass over Jordan this day, to go in to possess nations greater and mightier than thyself, cities great and fenced up to heaven."

teens.question: Moses listed multiple times Israel had provoked the LORD. Name two of the rebellions he recalled.
teens.option_a: The golden calf at Horeb, the rebellion at Taberah, Massah, Kibroth-hattaavah, and Kadesh-barnea
teens.option_b: Crossing the Red Sea and camping at Sinai
teens.option_c: The complaints about manna and the attack on Amalek
teens.option_d: The rebellion of Korah and the incident at Baal-peor
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:22-23 — "And at Taberah, and at Massah, and at Kibroth-hattaavah, ye provoked the LORD to wrath...Also in Kadesh-barnea ye rebelled."

teens.question: What drastic action did Moses take with the two tablets when he came down and saw the golden calf?
teens.option_a: He hid them in the ark
teens.option_b: He cast them out of his two hands and brake them before their eyes
teens.option_c: He raised them above his head and called Israel to repent
teens.option_d: He gave them to the priests for safekeeping
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:17 — "And I took the two tables, and cast them out of my two hands, and brake them before your eyes."

teens.question: How did Moses destroy the golden calf?
teens.option_a: He burnt it with fire, beat it to dust, and cast the dust into the brook
teens.option_b: He melted it and threw it in the river
teens.option_c: He buried it in the ground outside the camp
teens.option_d: He commanded the Levites to carry it away
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:21 — "And I took your sin, the calf which ye had made, and burnt it with fire, and stamped it, and ground it very small, even until it was as small as dust: and I cast the dust thereof into the brook."

teens.question: For whom did Moses pray during his forty days of intercession, according to the end of chapter 9?
teens.option_a: For Aaron whom the LORD was also angry with, and for all Israel
teens.option_b: For himself that he might enter the land
teens.option_c: For the Levites who did not sin
teens.option_d: For the next generation who would inherit the land
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:20 — "And the LORD was very angry with Aaron to have destroyed him: and I prayed for Aaron also at the same time."

adults.question: What theological principle does Moses establish in Deuteronomy 9:4-6 that is foundational for understanding grace?
adults.option_a: That Israel's possession of the land was not due to their righteousness but to God's grace and the nations' wickedness
adults.option_b: That righteousness is measured by obedience to law
adults.option_c: That God rewards military courage above all virtues
adults.option_d: That national prosperity comes from inherited merit
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:4-5 — "Not for thy righteousness, or for the uprightness of thine heart, dost thou go to possess their land: but for the wickedness of these nations."

adults.question: Moses describes Israel as a "stiffnecked people" in chapter 9. What does this agricultural metaphor convey?
adults.option_a: That Israel worked hard like oxen
adults.option_b: That like an ox that will not submit to the yoke, Israel was stubbornly resistant to God's direction
adults.option_c: That Israel's priests were too rigid in their interpretations
adults.option_d: That Israel was physically strong but spiritually lazy
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:6 — "Understand therefore, that the LORD thy God giveth thee not this good land for thy righteousness; for thou art a stiffnecked people."

adults.question: What was God's stated intention after the golden calf, and how did Moses intercede against it?
adults.option_a: God intended to scatter Israel and Moses prayed for mercy
adults.option_b: God said He would destroy Israel and make of Moses a nation, but Moses prostrated himself forty days praying for Israel
adults.option_c: God intended to send Israel back to Egypt and Moses argued this would shame God before Pharaoh
adults.option_d: God intended to give the land to Caleb only and Moses argued for all twelve tribes
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:14,18 — "Let me alone, that I may destroy them...And I fell down before the LORD...forty days and forty nights."

adults.question: Moses bases his intercessory prayer on several arguments. What is the primary theological argument he uses in chapter 9?
adults.option_a: That Israel had suffered enough in Egypt already
adults.option_b: That God should spare Israel lest the land from which He brought them say He brought them out to destroy them
adults.option_c: That the patriarchs had been perfectly righteous
adults.option_d: That the younger generation had not sinned
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:28 — "Lest the land whence thou broughtest us out say, Because the LORD was not able to bring them into the land...he hath brought them out to slay them."

adults.question: What does Moses' second forty-day fast on the mountain accomplish according to the narrative?
adults.option_a: God writes new commandments broader than the first
adults.option_b: God hears Moses and agrees not to destroy Israel, preparing for new tablets
adults.option_c: Moses receives the blueprints for the tabernacle
adults.option_d: God lifts the curse of wandering and promises immediate entry to Canaan
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:18-19 — "And I fell down before the LORD...and the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also."

adults.question: How does Moses describe the Anakims in chapter 9 in terms of their military reputation?
adults.option_a: As sons of the Anakims — tall and dreadful — of whom Israel had heard: Who can stand before the children of Anak?
adults.option_b: As skilled archers feared throughout Canaan
adults.option_c: As a people protected by powerful magic
adults.option_d: As chariot warriors superior to any in Egypt
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:2 — "A people great and tall, the children of the Anakims, whom thou knowest, and of whom thou hast heard say, Who can stand before the children of Anak!"

adults.question: What does the phrase "from the day thou didst depart out of the land of Egypt" in verse 7 communicate about Israel's track record?
adults.option_a: Israel had been provoking the LORD to wrath continuously from departure out of Egypt until that very day
adults.option_b: Israel had been mostly obedient with occasional failures
adults.option_c: Israel had improved significantly after the wilderness years
adults.option_d: Israel had been faithful in Egypt but rebellious only in the wilderness
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:7 — "Remember, and forget not, how thou provokedst the LORD thy God to wrath in the wilderness: from the day that thou didst depart out of the land of Egypt, until ye came unto this place, ye have been rebellious against the LORD."

adults.question: Moses' appeal to "thy servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" in his prayer establishes what hermeneutical principle for understanding divine faithfulness?
adults.option_a: That the patriarchs' merits transfer to their descendants
adults.option_b: That God's covenantal promises made to the fathers are irrevocable and form the ground of mercy for their descendants
adults.option_c: That intercessory prayer must always invoke ancestral precedents
adults.option_d: That God favors those with famous relatives
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 9:27 — "Remember thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; look not unto the stubbornness of this people, nor to their wickedness, nor to their sin."

kids.question: God told Moses to make a special box to keep the stone tablets in. What was it called?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:1-2 — "Hew thee two tables of stone...and make thee an ark of wood...and I will write on the tables the words that were in the first tables."

kids.question: God told Israel to love and care for which group of needy people, just as He cares for them?
kids.option_a: The stranger, the fatherless, and the widow
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:18-19 — "He doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow, and loveth the stranger...Love ye therefore the stranger."

kids.question: Moses said God was "God of gods" and "Lord of lords." What did he say about the size of the LORD?
kids.option_a: God was a great God, a mighty, and a terrible, which regardeth not persons
kids.option_b: God was as mighty as all the armies of the earth
kids.option_c: God was as high as the tallest mountain
kids.option_d: God was as wide as the seas
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:17 — "For the LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords, a great God, a mighty, and a terrible, which regardeth not persons, nor taketh reward."

teens.question: What did God ask of Israel in Deuteronomy 10:12, summarizing the entire law in a few words?
teens.option_a: To fear the LORD, walk in all his ways, love him, serve him with all heart and soul, and keep his commandments
teens.option_b: To build Him a temple and bring sacrifices daily
teens.option_c: To defeat all their enemies and possess the land
teens.option_d: To teach their children the law and keep the Sabbath
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:12 — "What doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the LORD thy God with all thy heart and with all thy soul."

teens.question: What does Moses mean by "circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart" in Deuteronomy 10:16?
teens.option_a: Undergo a new physical ceremony
teens.option_b: Remove spiritual stubbornness and pride — surrender the inner self to God
teens.option_c: Cut away contact with foreign nations
teens.option_d: Adopt a new form of worship
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:16 — "Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart, and be no more stiffnecked."

teens.question: What tribe did God set apart for special sacred service with no land inheritance among the tribes?
teens.option_a: The tribe of Levi
teens.option_b: The tribe of Judah
teens.option_c: The tribe of Reuben
teens.option_d: The tribe of Joseph
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:8-9 — "At that time the LORD separated the tribe of Levi, to bear the ark...The LORD is his inheritance."

teens.question: When Moses went back up the mountain for the second time, what did God command him to bring?
teens.option_a: Two tables of stone like the first and come up unto the LORD
teens.option_b: Offerings of gold and silver
teens.option_c: The elders of Israel
teens.option_d: The ark of the covenant
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:1 — "At that time the LORD said unto me, Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first, and come up unto me into the mount."

teens.question: Moses reminded Israel of how many people went down into Egypt with Jacob?
teens.option_a: Seventy souls
teens.option_b: One hundred and twenty souls
teens.option_c: Forty souls
teens.option_d: Two hundred souls
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:22 — "Thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven for multitude."

adults.question: What are the four specific duties of the Levites listed in Deuteronomy 10:8?
adults.option_a: To bear the ark of the covenant, to stand before the LORD to minister, to bless in his name, and these functions continue to Moses' day
adults.option_b: To teach the law, collect tithes, offer sacrifices, and judge cases
adults.option_c: To guard the tabernacle, lead worship, instruct the people, and arbitrate disputes
adults.option_d: To offer burnt offerings, perform the Passover, maintain the tabernacle, and sound the trumpets
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:8 — "The LORD separated the tribe of Levi...to bear the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD to minister unto him, and to bless in his name."

adults.question: What is the significance of God saying "the heaven and the heaven of heavens" belong to Him in verse 14, in the context of His choosing Israel?
adults.option_a: God owns the cosmos yet sovereignly condescended to delight in Israel's fathers and their descendants above all nations
adults.option_b: God is reminding Israel that He controls the weather and harvests
adults.option_c: God is warning Israel that angels watch their behaviour from heaven
adults.option_d: God is contrasting heavenly permanence with earthly transience
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:14-15 — "Behold, the heaven and the heaven of heavens is the LORD's...Only the LORD had a delight in thy fathers to love them."

adults.question: What specific attributes of God's impartiality does Moses cite in verse 17, and how does this ground the command to care for the stranger?
adults.option_a: God regardeth not persons nor taketh reward — He executes justice for fatherless and widow and loves the stranger — therefore Israel, once strangers in Egypt, must love the stranger
adults.option_b: God is omniscient and sees all injustice, therefore Israel must be just
adults.option_c: God is holy and Israel must be holy as He is holy
adults.option_d: God is merciful and Israel must show mercy to all peoples
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:17-19 — "God...regardeth not persons...he doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow, and loveth the stranger...Love ye therefore the stranger: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt."

adults.question: The command to "cleave unto him" in verse 20 uses the same Hebrew verb (dabaq) as Genesis 2:24's marriage union. What does this imply about Israel's covenant relationship?
adults.option_a: That covenant loyalty to God is modelled on the intimacy and permanence of marriage
adults.option_b: That God and Israel are literally married in a legal sense
adults.option_c: That breaking the covenant is like adultery
adults.option_d: That the patriarchs were priests who mediated God's presence like a husband
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:20 — "Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God; him shalt thou serve, and to him shalt thou cleave" — the verb cleave (dabaq) echoes the marital bond of Genesis 2:24.

adults.question: What location is given as the place where Aaron died in chapter 10?
adults.option_a: At Mosera
adults.option_b: At the waters of Meribah
adults.option_c: On Mount Hor
adults.option_d: At Kadesh-barnea
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:6 — "And the children of Israel took their journey from Beeroth of the children of Jaakan to Mosera: there Aaron died, and there he was buried."

adults.question: What phrase does Moses use in verse 21 to describe God's deeds, connecting back to the Exodus and forward to Israel's testimony?
adults.option_a: He is thy praise, and he is thy God, that hath done for thee these great and terrible things which thine eyes have seen
adults.option_b: He is the God of gods who made heaven and earth in six days
adults.option_c: He is the LORD of hosts who fights all Israel's battles
adults.option_d: He is the eternal One who changes not from generation to generation
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:21 — "He is thy praise, and he is thy God, that hath done for thee these great and terrible things, which thine eyes have seen."

adults.question: Moses transitions from recounting Israel's failures in chapter 9 to summarizing the covenant demands in chapter 10:12-13. What does this structural move communicate about the relationship between grace and obedience?
adults.option_a: Obedience earns God's forgiveness for past sins
adults.option_b: God's patient forgiveness (ch 9) is the basis and motivation for wholehearted obedience (ch 10:12-13)
adults.option_c: Moses was trying to frighten Israel with their past failures into future obedience
adults.option_d: The covenant demands are a punishment for rebellion
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:12 — "And now, Israel, what doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD...to walk in all his ways" — the "and now" follows the account of grace in chapter 9.

adults.question: The growth from seventy souls who went to Egypt to a multitude "as the stars of heaven" in verse 22 deliberately echoes which Abrahamic promise?
adults.option_a: The promise that Abraham's descendants would number as the sand of the sea
adults.option_b: The promise in Genesis 15:5 that Abraham's seed would be as numerous as the stars of heaven
adults.option_c: The promise that Jacob's sons would become twelve great nations
adults.option_d: The promise that Canaan would be filled with Israel's children
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 10:22 — "Thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven for multitude" — fulfilling Genesis 15:5 and 22:17.

kids.question: What did God promise to send in its season if Israel obeyed Him?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 11:14 — "That I will give you the rain of your land in his due season, the first rain and the latter rain, that thou mayest gather in thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil."

kids.question: What two mountains did Moses say would have a blessing on one and a curse on the other?
