adults.question: What does the phrase "thou shalt not respect persons" in Deuteronomy 24:17 establish as a legal principle for the vulnerable?
adults.option_a: Impartial justice must extend to the most powerless — stranger, fatherless, and widow — who lack advocates in the system
adults.option_b: Judges were permitted to favour Israelites over foreigners in disputed cases
adults.option_c: The law only required equal treatment between Israelite citizens
adults.option_d: Only religious courts were subject to the impartiality requirement
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 24:17 — "Thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless; nor take a widow's raiment to pledge." The law explicitly protects those most likely to be exploited by the powerful — the alien, the orphan, and the widow.

kids.question: What was the maximum number of stripes a judge could order for a wicked man?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:3 — "Forty stripes he may give him, and not exceed: lest, if he should exceed, and beat him above these with many stripes, then thy brother should seem vile unto thee."

kids.question: What were Israelites not allowed to do to an ox while it was treading out the grain?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:4 — "Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn."

kids.question: What were Israelites commanded to do to Amalek when they were settled in the land?
kids.option_a: Blot out the remembrance of Amalek
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:19 — "thou shalt blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; thou shalt not forget it."

teens.question: What was the levirate marriage law when a married man died without a son?
teens.option_a: His brother was to marry the widow to raise up seed in the dead brother's name
teens.option_b: The widow returned to her father's house for seven years
teens.option_c: The nearest male relative received the dead man's property but not his wife
teens.option_d: The widow could choose any man from the dead husband's tribe
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:5 — "If brethren dwell together, and one of them die, and have no child, the wife of the dead shall not marry without unto a stranger: her husband's brother shall go in unto her, and take her to him to wife, and perform the duty of an husband's brother unto her."

teens.question: What was the ceremony called when a man refused to marry his dead brother's wife?
teens.option_a: The ceremony of the removed sandal (halitzah)
teens.option_b: The reading of the law of release
teens.option_c: The presentation before the tabernacle
teens.option_d: The trial of the bitter waters
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:9-10 — "Then shall his brother's wife come unto him in the presence of the elders, and loose his shoe from off his foot, and spit in his face...And his name shall be called in Israel, The house of him that hath his shoe loosed."

teens.question: Why did Moses command Amalek to be blotted out?
teens.option_a: Because Amalek attacked the weakest Israelites at the rear when they came out of Egypt
teens.option_b: Because Amalek worshipped idols and led Israel into sin
teens.option_c: Because Amalek refused to give Israel food and water
teens.option_d: Because Amalek had enslaved Israelites before the exodus
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:17-18 — "Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way, when ye were come forth out of Egypt; How he met thee by the way, and smote the hindmost of thee, even all that were feeble behind thee...and he feared not God."

teens.question: What was the penalty for a woman who grabbed a man's genitals while he was fighting with her husband?
teens.option_a: Her hand was to be cut off without pity
teens.option_b: She paid a fine of ten shekels
teens.option_c: She was publicly shamed before the elders
teens.option_d: She was put out of the city
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:11-12 — "When men strive together one with another, and the wife of the one draweth near for to deliver her husband out of the hand of him that smiteth him, and putteth forth her hand, and taketh him by the secrets: Then thou shalt cut off her hand, thine eye shall not pity her."

teens.question: What was Israel commanded to use in commerce according to Deuteronomy 25?
teens.option_a: A just and perfect weight and measure
teens.option_b: Weights approved by the Levites only
teens.option_c: Silver coins struck by the king
teens.option_d: Grain measured at the tabernacle
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:15 — "But thou shalt have a perfect and just weight, a perfect and just measure shalt thou have: that thy days may be lengthened in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee."

adults.question: How does Paul apply Deuteronomy 25:4 ("Thou shalt not muzzle the ox") in 1 Corinthians 9:9-10 and 1 Timothy 5:18?
adults.option_a: Paul argues that if God cared enough to legislate for working animals, he certainly intends workers in the gospel to be financially supported
adults.option_b: Paul uses it to argue that all physical labour must be rewarded equally
adults.option_c: Paul only cites it as a historical example of Mosaic law that has been cancelled
adults.option_d: Paul uses it to argue that animals should be treated equally to humans
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 9:9-10 — "Doth God take care for oxen? Or saith he it altogether for our sakes?...he that thresheth in hope should be partaker of his hope." Paul uses the ox law as a fortiori argument: if animals merit their food while working, gospel ministers deserve material support.

adults.question: What principle of human dignity underlies the forty-stripe limit in Deuteronomy 25:3?
adults.option_a: Even a lawfully condemned criminal retains the dignity of being a brother; punishment has a limit beyond which it becomes dehumanizing
adults.option_b: The limit was set to prevent the condemned from dying
adults.option_c: The rule was primarily about deterrence — fewer stripes were more feared
adults.option_d: The limit applied only to minor offences; major crimes had no cap
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:3 — "lest...thy brother should seem vile unto thee." The convicted wrongdoer is still "thy brother." The text locates the limit not in the criminal's physical tolerance but in the community's obligation to honour human worth.

adults.question: What purpose did levirate marriage serve in the context of Israel's covenant theology and land inheritance?
adults.option_a: It kept family names and land holdings intact within tribal lines, preserving the covenant gift of the land across generations
adults.option_b: It was primarily a welfare mechanism to care for widows financially
adults.option_c: It ensured that Israelite women were never without a husband
adults.option_d: It was a way of pooling tribal resources during times of war
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:6 — "that the firstborn which she beareth shall succeed in the name of his brother which is dead, that his name be not put out of Israel." Name = inheritance = tribal land portion. Levirate marriage prevented both the name and land from passing out of the family.

adults.question: How does the book of Ruth illustrate and develop the levirate principle of Deuteronomy 25, and how does Boaz's role differ?
adults.option_a: Ruth illustrates a near-kinsman's refusal and a more distant kinsman (Boaz) freely embracing the redemption duty out of covenant love
adults.option_b: Ruth shows that levirate marriage was obsolete by the time of the judges
adults.option_c: Ruth shows that only brothers could perform levirate duty
adults.option_d: Ruth demonstrates that women could refuse the arrangement entirely
adults.explanation: Ruth 4:6-10 — The nearest kinsman refused, then Boaz redeemed Ruth and Naomi's land. Boaz was a kinsman-redeemer (go'el), going beyond strict legal obligation out of hesed (covenant love), making him a type of Christ who redeems beyond what law required.

adults.question: What does the abhorrence of dishonest weights (25:13-16) reveal about God's character in relation to economic life?
adults.option_a: God is a God of justice who hates fraud; dishonest weights are "abomination unto the LORD" — the same term used of idolatry
adults.option_b: Commercial fraud was a lower category of sin than ritual violation
adults.option_c: The law applied only in the land of Canaan, not in the wilderness or exile
adults.option_d: Only merchants and traders were required to use honest weights
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:16 — "For all that do such things, and all that do unrighteously, are an abomination unto the LORD thy God." Proverbs 11:1 — "A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight." Commercial integrity is a direct expression of the fear of God.

adults.question: What does the phrase "he feared not God" in Deuteronomy 25:18 identify as the root cause of Amalek's attack on the vulnerable?
adults.option_a: The fear of God is the only reliable restraint on brutality; those without it have no limit to cruelty against the weak
adults.option_b: Amalek feared other gods but not the LORD specifically
adults.option_c: The phrase means Amalek was militarily overconfident
adults.option_d: It is a legal formula used to justify capital punishment
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:18 — "and he feared not God." The attacking of the "feeble behind thee" — the sick, the elderly, the exhausted — is traced not to strategic calculation but to spiritual rootlessness. Fear of God is the foundation of ethical restraint.

adults.question: How does the Sadducees' question in Matthew 22:23-28 about the woman with seven husbands relate to the levirate law of Deuteronomy 25?
adults.option_a: The Sadducees used the law to argue that only the first husband counted in the resurrection
adults.option_b: The Sadducees constructed an absurd case under levirate law to argue that resurrection was logically impossible
adults.option_c: The Sadducees sought to know which levirate marriage was legally valid
adults.option_d: The Sadducees were testing Jesus' knowledge of rabbinical tradition
adults.explanation: Matthew 22:25-28 — "Now there were with us seven brethren: and the first...died, and...left his wife unto his brother...In the resurrection therefore, whose wife shall she be?" The Sadducees used Deuteronomy 25 as a reductio ad absurdum against resurrection. Jesus corrected their misunderstanding of both resurrection and Scripture.

adults.question: What does the sandal ceremony in Deuteronomy 25:9-10 communicate about name, inheritance, and public shame in Israelite society?
adults.option_a: Removing the sandal publicly symbolised the stripping away of a man's standing, right of redemption, and legacy for failing covenant responsibility
adults.option_b: The sandal was given to the widow as compensation for the loss of her inheritance
adults.option_c: The ceremony absolved the man of all further obligation to his brother's household
adults.option_d: It was purely a formal legal discharge with no social stigma attached
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 25:10 — "And his name shall be called in Israel, The house of him that hath his shoe loosed." In ancient Near Eastern culture, sandal transfer signified the cession of legal rights (cf. Ruth 4:7-8). The ceremony turned private legal refusal into public shame, embedding social accountability.

kids.question: What were Israelites to bring to the priest when they first harvested their crops in the promised land?
kids.option_a: The firstfruits of the ground in a basket
kids.option_b: The tenth of all their grain
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:2 — "That thou shalt take of the first of all the fruit of the earth...and shalt put it in a basket, and shalt go unto the place which the LORD thy God shall choose to place his name there."

kids.question: What did the Israelite recite when he brought his firstfruits to the priest?
kids.option_a: A historical confession beginning: "A Syrian ready to perish was my father"
kids.option_d: A prayer for the coming harvest
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:5 — "And thou shalt speak and say before the LORD thy God, A Syrian ready to perish was my father, and he went down into Egypt."

kids.question: Who were the three groups that received a share of the tithe every third year?
kids.option_a: The Levite, the stranger, and the fatherless and widow
kids.option_b: The priests, the poor, and the elders
kids.option_c: The warriors, the widows, and the Levites
kids.option_d: The sick, the strangers, and the orphans
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:12-13 — "thou shalt give it unto the Levite, the stranger, the fatherless, and the widow, that they may eat within thy gates, and be filled."

teens.question: What happened to the firstfruits basket after it was presented to the priest?
teens.option_a: The priest set it down before the altar of the LORD
teens.option_b: It was burned on the altar as a whole offering
teens.option_c: The Levites divided it among themselves
teens.option_d: It was sent to the storehouse for the poor
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:4 — "And the priest shall take the basket out of thine hand, and set it down before the altar of the LORD thy God."

teens.question: In the firstfruits liturgy, what did the Israelite confess about God's response to Israel's suffering in Egypt?
teens.option_a: That the LORD heard their voice and looked on their affliction and brought them out with great signs and wonders
teens.option_b: That the LORD sent plagues only against Pharaoh but not Egypt's people
teens.option_c: That God waited until the fourth generation to act as he promised Abraham
teens.option_d: That God sent Moses to negotiate Israel's release through peaceful diplomacy
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:7-8 — "And when we cried unto the LORD God of our fathers, the LORD heard our voice, and looked on our affliction, and our labour, and our oppression: And the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt with a mighty hand...and with great terribleness, and with signs, and with wonders."

teens.question: What declaration did an Israelite make at the end of the tithe in the third year?
teens.option_a: That he had obeyed all God's commands regarding the tithe and had not eaten it while unclean or used it for the dead
teens.option_b: That he had paid the full amount and no less
teens.option_c: That he had divided it equally among all the poor
teens.option_d: That the tithe was a freewill offering and not under compulsion
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:13-14 — "I have...not transgressed thy commandments, neither have I forgotten them: I have not eaten thereof in my mourning, neither have I taken away ought thereof for any unclean use, nor given ought thereof for the dead."

teens.question: What two things did God declare about Israel in the covenant ratification at the end of Deuteronomy 26?
teens.option_a: God declared Israel to be his peculiar people above all nations, and Israel declared the LORD to be their God
teens.option_b: God promised the land forever and Israel promised to keep the law forever
teens.option_c: God promised rain in its season and Israel promised to bring the full tithe
teens.option_d: God declared Israel holy and Israel declared the law to be perfect
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:17-18 — "Thou hast avouched the LORD this day to be thy God...And the LORD hath avouched thee this day to be his peculiar people, as he hath promised thee, and that thou shouldest keep all his commandments."

teens.question: What was the purpose of the firstfruits ceremony in terms of Israel's identity?
teens.option_a: It was a liturgical act of gratitude that retold the story of redemption and tied each generation to God's saving acts
teens.option_b: It was purely an agricultural tax to support the tabernacle
teens.option_c: It demonstrated that every Israelite had a surplus to give
teens.option_d: It was a way of testing whether each household had prospered
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:5-10 — The entire history from wandering ancestor to exodus to promised land was recited at the offering. This made worship a retelling of identity, not merely a transaction.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the phrase "A Syrian ready to perish was my father" (26:5) in the firstfruits liturgy?
adults.option_a: It grounded Israel's identity in vulnerability and divine rescue, not in ethnic superiority — they began as a nearly-lost wanderer
adults.option_b: It identified Abraham as the ancestor of the Syrians
adults.option_c: It referred to Jacob's time as a servant in Aram working for Laban
adults.option_d: It was a confession of Israel's sin in intermarrying with Syrians
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:5 — "A Syrian ready to perish was my father." The Hebrew arami oved — a perishing Aramean — refers to Jacob who sojourned in Aram. Beginning the creed with this confession of weakness and wandering made grace, not achievement, the foundation of Israelite identity.

adults.question: What does the tithe declaration of Deuteronomy 26:13-15 reveal about the relationship between worship and social care?
adults.option_a: True worship involves both ritual obedience and care for the vulnerable; the tithe declaration integrates these as inseparable
adults.option_b: Social care was a separate track from worship with no liturgical expression
adults.option_c: The declaration was purely about ensuring the tithe went to the right institutions
adults.option_d: Worship was prioritised over social care; the tithe to the poor was secondary
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:13 — "I have...given it unto the Levite, and unto the stranger, to the fatherless, and to the widow, according to all thy commandments." Then the worshipper asks God to "look down from thy holy habitation" and bless the people. Obedient social care was the condition for divine blessing.

adults.question: How does the mutual avowal formula of Deuteronomy 26:17-19 function as a covenant treaty compared to ancient Near Eastern suzerain-vassal treaties?
adults.option_a: Both parties make reciprocal declarations, but unlike human treaties where only the vassal vows, here God also vows to acknowledge Israel — making it a covenant of mutual obligation
adults.option_b: The formula matches Hittite vassal treaties exactly where only the vassal spoke
adults.option_c: It was a one-sided divine promise with no human obligation
adults.option_d: The avowal was purely liturgical with no legal force
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:17-18 — Israel avouched the LORD, and the LORD avouched Israel. Unlike pagan suzerain treaties where only the lesser party pledged, this mutual avowal elevated Israel to the unique status of covenant partner, not merely subject.

adults.question: What does the phrase "holy people" (am qadosh) in Deuteronomy 26:19 mean in its covenant context?
adults.option_a: Set apart to the LORD — consecrated for his purposes and reflecting his character among the nations
adults.option_b: Morally perfect and sinless before God
adults.option_c: Racially distinct from all other peoples
adults.option_d: Entitled to special treatment above all other humans
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:19 — "to make thee high above all nations which he hath made...and that thou mayest be an holy people unto the LORD thy God." Qadosh (holy) means set apart. Israel's holiness was vocational — called to display God's character to the nations, not to assert superiority.

adults.question: How does the firstfruits liturgy of Deuteronomy 26 function as a model for Christian confession and Eucharistic anamnesis?
adults.option_a: It models the pattern of reciting God's saving acts in history as the basis for present worship — a pattern fulfilled in the Lord's Supper's "Do this in remembrance of me"
adults.option_b: It was completely replaced by the New Testament and has no continuing significance
adults.option_c: It only applied in the land of Israel and had no universal application
adults.option_d: It served as a model only for Jewish synagogue liturgy, not Christian worship
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:5-10 — The pattern: recite God's saving acts → present the offering → worship. This "narrated offering" pattern is foundational to Christian liturgy: we rehearse the gospel before receiving Communion, just as Israel rehearsed the exodus before bringing firstfruits.

adults.question: What does the tithe declaration's prohibition on using the tithe "for the dead" (26:14) reveal about Israelite faith versus Canaanite practice?
adults.option_a: Canaanite religion included feeding the dead (marzeah feasts); Israel's law forbade mixing covenant resources with the cult of the dead
adults.option_b: The prohibition was purely hygienic — dead bodies contaminated food
adults.option_c: It meant that money could not be spent on funerals from the tithe
adults.option_d: The prohibition applied only when mourning for idolaters
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:14 — "neither have I given ought thereof for the dead." Archaeological evidence from Ugarit confirms marzeah feasts where food was offered to ancestral spirits. Israel's tithes were for the living — the Levite, stranger, orphan, widow — not the dead.

adults.question: What is the significance of the firstfruits liturgy being placed immediately before the covenant ceremony on Ebal and Gerizim in Deuteronomy 27?
adults.option_a: Gratitude and confession of God's acts (ch. 26) naturally lead into covenant commitment (ch. 27); worship precedes and motivates obedience
adults.option_b: The placement was accidental based on oral tradition assembly
adults.option_c: Chapter 27 corrects errors in the firstfruits law by adding the Ebal ceremony
adults.option_d: The firstfruits offering had to be completed before any covenant ceremony could be valid
adults.explanation: The sequence of Deuteronomy 26-27 moves from worshipping gratitude (firstfruits and tithe, recalling salvation history) to covenant renewal (Ebal/Gerizim stones and altar). The theological logic is: memory of grace → liturgical response → covenant commitment.

adults.question: How does God's description of the promised land in Deuteronomy 26:15 as "a land flowing with milk and honey" relate to the firstfruits offering?
adults.option_a: The worshipper's firstfruits are the concrete evidence that God has kept his promise — the gift of the land is acknowledged by offering back its first produce
adults.option_b: The phrase "milk and honey" was purely metaphorical and not connected to actual agricultural produce
adults.option_c: The description was used only in Moses' prayer and had no relation to the offering
adults.option_d: God used the phrase to contrast Canaan's fertility with Egypt's poverty
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 26:15 — "look down from thy holy habitation, from heaven, and bless thy people Israel, and the land which thou hast given us, as thou swarest unto our fathers, a land that floweth with milk and honey." The firstfruits ceremony closes the theological loop: God promised milk and honey; the basket of firstfruits is Israel's testimony that he delivered.

kids.question: On which mountain were the Israelites told to set up large plastered stones with the law written on them?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:4 — "And it shall be when ye be gone over Jordan, that ye shall set up these stones, which I command thee this day, in mount Ebal, and thou shalt plaister them with plaister."

kids.question: What kind of altar did Moses say Israel must build on Mount Ebal?
kids.option_a: An altar of whole stones on which no iron tool had been used
kids.option_b: An altar made of cedar wood from Lebanon
kids.option_c: A bronze altar like the one in the tabernacle
kids.option_d: An altar built from stones taken from the Jordan River
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:5-6 — "thou shalt build an altar unto the LORD thy God, an altar of stones: thou shalt not lift up any iron tool upon them. Thou shalt build the altar of the LORD thy God of whole stones."

kids.question: What were all the people to say after each curse was pronounced by the Levites?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:15 — "the Levites shall speak, and say...And all the people shall answer and say, Amen." This pattern of Amen is repeated twelve times through the curses.

teens.question: What two kinds of offerings were to be sacrificed on the altar at Mount Ebal?
teens.option_a: Burnt offerings and peace offerings
teens.option_b: Sin offerings and burnt offerings
teens.option_c: Thank offerings and trespass offerings
teens.option_d: Firstfruits and wave offerings
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:6-7 — "Thou shalt build the altar of the LORD thy God of whole stones: and thou shalt offer burnt offerings thereon unto the LORD thy God: And thou shalt offer peace offerings, and shalt eat there, and rejoice before the LORD thy God."

teens.question: Which six tribes stood on Mount Gerizim to bless the people?
teens.option_a: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin
teens.option_b: Reuben, Gad, Dan, Naphtali, Asher, and Zebulun
teens.option_c: Levi, Judah, Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin, and Dan
teens.option_d: All twelve tribes stood on Gerizim with six on each side
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:12 — "These shall stand upon mount Gerizim to bless the people, when ye are come over Jordan; Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Joseph, and Benjamin."

teens.question: Which six tribes stood on Mount Ebal for the cursing?
teens.option_a: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali
teens.option_b: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin
teens.option_c: Dan, Zebulun, Naphtali, Asher, Ephraim, and Manasseh
teens.option_d: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Gad, Asher, and Dan
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:13 — "And these shall stand upon mount Ebal to curse; Reuben, Gad, and Asher, and Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali."

teens.question: How many curses were pronounced by the Levites on Mount Ebal?
teens.option_a: Twelve
teens.option_b: Ten
teens.option_c: Seven
teens.option_d: Twenty-four
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:15-26 — The text lists twelve specific curses, each followed by the congregation's Amen, concluding with the summary curse in verse 26.

teens.question: What was the last and most comprehensive curse pronounced at Mount Ebal?
teens.option_a: "Cursed be he that confirmeth not all the words of this law to do them"
teens.option_b: "Cursed be he that taketh any reward to slay an innocent person"
teens.option_c: "Cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark"
teens.option_d: "Cursed be he that maketh any graven or molten image"
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:26 — "Cursed be he that confirmeth not all the words of this law to do them. And all the people shall say, Amen." Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:10 to argue that the law curses all who do not keep it perfectly.

adults.question: Why were uncut (whole) stones required for the altar at Ebal, and what theological principle does this embody?
adults.option_a: Human craftsmanship would profane what was consecrated to God; the altar was to come to God as he made it, not improved by human skill
adults.option_b: Iron tools were considered ritually unclean after contact with Canaanite worship
adults.option_c: The law was simply practical — cut stones were harder to assemble quickly
adults.option_d: Uncut stones represented the twelve tribes in their natural state
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:5 — "thou shalt not lift up any iron tool upon them." Exodus 20:25 gives the same instruction. God's altar was not to be "polluted" by human enhancement. The principle: what is consecrated to God does not require human improvement to be acceptable.

adults.question: How does Paul apply the final curse of Deuteronomy 27:26 in Galatians 3:10-13?
adults.option_a: Paul argues that everyone who relies on the law is under a curse since none can keep it perfectly; only Christ who became a curse for us redeems from it
adults.option_b: Paul uses it to argue that the Torah was never meant to be obeyed literally
adults.option_c: Paul says the curse only applied to Israelites who had the law, not to Gentiles
adults.option_d: Paul argues the curse is broken by sincere faith even without perfect obedience
adults.explanation: Galatians 3:10 — "For as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse: for it is written, Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them." Paul uses the totality clause of Deuteronomy 27:26 to argue the law's comprehensive demand proves no one is justified by it.

adults.question: What is the symbolic significance of setting up the stones on Mount Ebal — the mountain of cursing — rather than Mount Gerizim?
adults.option_a: The law is written against the backdrop of the curse; Israel stands under judgment unless the covenant is kept — the law is framed by its sanctions, not its blessings
adults.option_b: It was purely topographical — Ebal was larger and more visible
adults.option_c: The law was placed on Ebal because it recorded the curses only, not the blessings
adults.option_d: The placement showed that Mount Ebal was the more sacred location
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:4 — "ye shall set up these stones...in mount Ebal." The law is written on the mountain associated with cursing, signalling that the covenant document must be understood within the gravity of its sanctions. Blessing was possible but the default of disobedience was curse.

adults.question: What does the selection of twelve curses in Deuteronomy 27 reveal about the structure of the covenant law?
adults.option_a: The twelve curses correspond structurally to the twelve tribes, implying each tribe was corporately implicated and responsible for the covenant's integrity
adults.option_b: The twelve curses cover only the twelve most common Israelite sins
adults.option_c: The number twelve was chosen because the Levitical code had twelve sections
adults.option_d: The curses were selected by lot from a longer list
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:15-26 lists twelve curses, one for each tribe. The structure is not coincidental — all Israel, represented by each tribe, affirmed the curses with Amen. The covenant obligation was total and corporate.

adults.question: What categories of sin do the twelve curses of Deuteronomy 27 primarily address?
adults.option_a: Idolatry, dishonour of parents, boundary violations, sexual immorality, violence against the vulnerable, and perversion of justice — sins often committed in secret
adults.option_b: Only public, high-handed sins that required capital punishment
adults.option_c: Exclusively ritual violations like unclean offerings and Sabbath breaking
adults.option_d: Economic crimes including theft, fraud, and debt default
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:15-26 — The curses target secret sins (idolatry in secret, sexual sins, killing for hire) and sins against the vulnerable (the blind, the stranger, the fatherless, the widow). Many are sins that courts could not easily detect — which is why the divine curse was invoked.

adults.question: How does the Ebal/Gerizim ceremony in Joshua 8:30-35 demonstrate Joshua's faithfulness to Moses' command in Deuteronomy 27?
adults.option_a: Joshua built the altar of unhewn stones, wrote the law on plaster stones, and assembled all Israel to hear the blessings and curses, exactly as Moses commanded
adults.option_b: Joshua built the altar but modified the ceremony to include a burnt offering for each tribe
adults.option_c: Joshua waited until after the conquest of Canaan was complete before performing the ceremony
adults.option_d: Joshua performed a simplified version of the ceremony using only the altar, omitting the stones
adults.explanation: Joshua 8:30-35 — "Then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal...And he wrote there upon the stones a copy of the law of Moses...And afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law." Every detail of Deuteronomy 27 was fulfilled.

adults.question: What does the practice of writing the law on plastered stones "very plainly" (27:8) establish about the relationship between law and literacy in Israel?
adults.option_a: The law was to be publicly visible, accessible, and readable — its public inscription was integral to Israel's accountability under covenant
adults.option_b: Only scribes and priests needed to read the law; the public display was ceremonial
adults.option_c: The inscribed stones served as legal evidence in border disputes
adults.option_d: The practice was borrowed from Egyptian stele traditions with no theological significance
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:8 — "thou shalt write upon the stones all the words of this law very plainly." The word "plainly" (ba'er — to engrave clearly) is the root of the word used in Deuteronomy 1:5 where Moses "expounded" the law. The law's clarity and public availability was a covenant requirement.

adults.question: What is the hermeneutical function of Deuteronomy 27 placed between Moses' ethical exhortations (chs. 12-26) and the blessings and curses (ch. 28)?
adults.option_a: It is the covenant-sealing ceremony that converts the legal code into binding oath — Israel's Amen transforms instruction into sworn obligation before God
adults.option_b: It is a historical interlude providing practical instructions for the conquest
adults.option_c: It marks the transition from Moses' first speech to his second speech
adults.option_d: It recapitulates the law for the benefit of tribes that had not yet heard it
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 27:9 — "Take heed, and hearken, O Israel; this day thou art become the people of the LORD thy God." Chapters 12-26 lay out the law; chapter 27 transforms that law into covenant through corporate ceremony; chapter 28 then announces the consequences. The structure is: instruction → ratification → sanctions.

kids.question: What would God do to Israel if they obeyed all his commandments?
kids.option_a: Set them high above all nations of the earth
kids.option_b: Give them a king like all the nations
kids.option_d: Send them back to the promised land from Egypt
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:1 — "And it shall come to pass, if thou shalt hearken diligently unto the voice of the LORD thy God...the LORD thy God will set thee on high above all nations of the earth."

kids.question: Moses said that if Israel obeyed God, what would happen to their enemies who attacked them?
kids.option_a: They would flee in seven directions
kids.option_b: They would become servants of Israel
kids.option_c: God would destroy them with fire from heaven
kids.option_d: They would never reach Israel's borders
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:7 — "The LORD shall cause thine enemies that rise up against thee to be smitten before thy face: they shall come out against thee one way, and flee before thee seven ways."

kids.question: What did Moses say would happen to Israel if they did not obey God — what kind of illness would strike them?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:22 — "The LORD shall smite thee with a consumption, and with a fever, and with an inflammation, and with an extreme burning, and with the sword, and with blasting, and with mildew."

teens.question: How many blessings of obedience does Deuteronomy 28 describe before turning to the curses?
teens.option_a: Fourteen verses of blessings (verses 1-14)
teens.option_b: Seven verses
teens.option_c: Twenty-one verses
teens.option_d: Forty verses
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:1-14 — The blessings occupy verses 1-14, covering every sphere of life: city and field, children and crops, enemies and reputation among nations.

teens.question: What did Moses say would happen to Israel's children, animals, and harvests if they obeyed God?
teens.option_a: All would be blessed — children, herds, crops in field and in storage
teens.option_b: The children would be wise and the harvest double
teens.option_c: Animals would multiply beyond counting
teens.option_d: The land would yield seven-fold harvests
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:4-5 — "Blessed shall be the fruit of thy body, and the fruit of thy ground, and the fruit of thy cattle...Blessed shall be thy basket and thy store."

teens.question: What terrible sickness among the curses would consume from the sole of the foot to the top of the head?
teens.option_a: A sore botch (the boil of Egypt) for which there was no healing
teens.option_b: Leprosy spreading over the whole body
teens.option_c: Blindness and madness together
teens.option_d: A burning fever that never left
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:35 — "The LORD shall smite thee in the knees, and in the legs, with a sore botch that cannot be healed, from the sole of thy foot unto the top of thy head."

teens.question: What siege condition did Moses describe as so terrible that parents would eat their own children?
teens.option_a: The siege laid by enemies during which Israel's food would be completely cut off
teens.option_b: The famine of the seventh year when the land rested
teens.option_c: The plague that struck during the time of the judges
teens.option_d: The drought God would send as punishment for idolatry
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:53 — "And thou shalt eat the fruit of thine own body, the flesh of thy sons and of thy daughters...in the straitness and in the siege, wherewith thine enemies shall distress thee."

teens.question: What did Moses predict would happen to Israel's population if they disobeyed God?
teens.option_a: They would be left few in number whereas they had been as the stars of heaven
teens.option_b: They would be scattered to the four winds
teens.option_c: Half would perish in battle and half in plague
teens.option_d: Each tribe would be reduced to a remnant of ten
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:62 — "And ye shall be left few in number, whereas ye were as the stars of heaven for multitude; because thou wouldest not obey the voice of the LORD thy God."

adults.question: What is the structural proportion of blessings to curses in Deuteronomy 28, and what does this asymmetry communicate theologically?
adults.option_a: Fourteen verses of blessings versus fifty-four verses of curses; the disproportion emphasises the gravity of covenant unfaithfulness and the accumulated consequences of abandoning God
adults.option_b: The curses are longer because they include more specific agricultural details
adults.option_c: The ratio reflects the probability of Israel's disobedience in Moses' prediction
adults.option_d: The curses are longer because they were written by a different author than the blessings
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:1-14 (blessings, 14 verses) vs. 28:15-68 (curses, 54 verses). The overwhelming weight of the curses is not pessimism but a prophetic warning: the covenant's default trajectory without sustained faithfulness is toward curse, not blessing.

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 28:25 reverse the imagery of 28:7 (enemies fleeing seven ways), and what does this reversal signify?
adults.option_a: Verse 7 promises enemies flee in seven ways before Israel; verse 25 pronounces Israel will flee in seven ways before enemies — disobedience reverses every covenant blessing into its mirror curse
adults.option_b: The two verses describe different battles at different times in Israel's history
adults.option_c: Verse 25 is a general curse unrelated to verse 7
adults.option_d: The reversal applies only to the northern kingdom of Israel
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:7 — "they shall come out against thee one way, and flee before thee seven ways." Deuteronomy 28:25 — "thou shalt go out one way against them, and flee seven ways before them." The blessings and curses are mirror structures — obedience and disobedience produce exact inversions.

adults.question: What historical fulfilment do scholars identify in the Egyptian exile predicted in Deuteronomy 28:68?
adults.option_a: After the fall of Jerusalem, Jewish refugees fled to Egypt (Jeremiah 42-44), and many were taken or sold there — reversing the exodus
adults.option_b: The verse refers to the Babylonian captivity which resembled Egypt in its slavery
adults.option_c: The prediction was fulfilled when Israelites voluntarily migrated to Alexandria for trade
adults.option_d: The verse is metaphorical and refers to spiritual slavery, not literal Egypt
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:68 — "And the LORD shall bring thee into Egypt again with ships...and there ye shall be sold unto your enemies for bondmen and bondwomen." Jeremiah 43:7 records the remnant fleeing to Egypt against God's command after Jerusalem's fall, fulfilling the reversal of the exodus.

adults.question: What does Deuteronomy 28:36-37 predict about Israel's kings and its role among the nations, and when was this fulfilled?
adults.option_a: Israel and her king would be exiled to a nation they did not know and become a byword and astonishment — fulfilled in the Assyrian and Babylonian exiles
adults.option_b: The prediction referred to Solomon's foreign wives and their influence
adults.option_c: The prediction was fulfilled when Saul was rejected as king
adults.option_d: The verse describes the Roman occupation of Judea in the New Testament period
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:36-37 — "The LORD shall bring thee, and thy king which thou shalt set over thee, unto a nation which neither thou nor thy fathers have known...thou shalt become an astonishment, a proverb, and a byword." 2 Kings 17 (Assyrian exile of Israel) and 2 Kings 25 (Babylonian exile of Judah) fulfilled this precisely.

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 28:64-67 ("thou shalt find no ease...a trembling heart, and failing of eyes, and sorrow of mind") describe the psychological condition of exile?
adults.option_a: It describes the existential anguish of displacement — no security, constant fear, no assurance of life from morning to evening — a portrait of covenant homelessness
adults.option_b: It describes the physical hardships of the journey into exile
adults.option_c: It applies only to those who personally participated in idolatry
adults.option_d: It describes the condition of Israelites who chose to remain in the land during captivity
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:65-67 — "among these nations shalt thou find no ease, neither shall the sole of thy foot have rest...In the morning thou shalt say, Would God it were even! and at even thou shalt say, Would God it were morning!" The curse extends beyond physical suffering to the dissolution of hope and security.

adults.question: What does the phrase "because thou servedst not the LORD thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart" (28:47) identify as the root cause of all the curses?
adults.option_a: Joyless, reluctant, or mechanical obedience to God — worship without heart — is the root failure that opens the door to all covenant curses
adults.option_b: Israel's failure to perform sacrifices with the correct rituals
adults.option_c: Israel's neglect of the sabbatical year and jubilee laws
adults.option_d: Israel's intermarriage with Canaanite nations
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:47-48 — "Because thou servedst not the LORD thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart...Therefore shalt thou serve thine enemies." Joyless obedience — doing the right things without love for God — was the seed of apostasy. True covenant faithfulness required the heart, not only the hand.

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 28:58 ("If thou wilt not observe to do all the words of this law...that thou mayest fear this glorious and fearful name, THE LORD THY GOD") summarise the theological foundation of all the blessings and curses?
adults.option_a: The fear of God's name — reverence for who God is — is the motivating centre of covenant obedience; law-keeping divorced from the fear of the LORD misses the point entirely
adults.option_b: The verse says the curses apply only to those who openly blaspheme God's name
adults.option_c: It establishes that knowledge of the law is sufficient even without obedience
adults.option_d: It is a summary formula for the priests to recite at annual festivals
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 28:58 — "this glorious and fearful name." The Name encapsulates God's character, power, and covenant faithfulness. The entire legal structure of Deuteronomy 12-28 rests on the foundation of fearing and honoring who God is — not merely obeying rules.

adults.question: In what way does Deuteronomy 28 function as a prophetic document, and how does Paul use its themes in Romans 9-11?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy 28 prophesied Israel's exile; Paul uses the hardening and partial rejection of Israel as a mystery within God's sovereign plan, with hope of full restoration
adults.option_b: Paul argues Deuteronomy 28 proves Israel permanently forfeited covenant status
adults.option_c: Paul uses Deuteronomy 28 only to argue for the curse of the law, not for restoration
adults.option_d: Deuteronomy 28 is treated by Paul as fulfilled entirely in the Babylonian exile with no ongoing significance
adults.explanation: Romans 11:25-26 — "blindness in part is happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in. And so all Israel shall be saved." Paul holds together the curses of Deuteronomy 28 (partial rejection) with the restorative promises of Deuteronomy 30 (full restoration), arguing both are within God's sovereign redemptive plan.

kids.question: What did Moses say Israel had seen with their own eyes during the forty years in the wilderness about their clothes and sandals?
kids.option_a: Their clothes and sandals had not worn out
kids.option_b: God had given them new garments each year
kids.option_c: Their clothes had been kept clean by God
kids.option_d: The LORD replaced their sandals at each new camp
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:5 — "And I have led you forty years in the wilderness: your clothes are not waxen old upon you, and thy shoe is not waxen old upon thy foot."

kids.question: What two things did Moses say Israel did not eat or drink in the wilderness so they would know God was the LORD?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:6 — "Ye have not eaten bread, neither have ye drunk wine or strong drink: that ye might know that I am the LORD your God."

kids.question: What does Deuteronomy 29:29 say belongs to God?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:29 — "The secret things belong unto the LORD our God: but those things which are revealed belong unto us and to our children for ever, that we may do all the words of this law."

teens.question: Where was the covenant in Deuteronomy 29 made, and how was it different from the Horeb covenant?
teens.option_a: In the land of Moab, in addition to the covenant God made at Horeb
teens.option_b: Exclusively in Moab, replacing the Horeb covenant entirely
teens.option_c: In Moab, reaffirming only the ten commandments of Horeb
teens.option_d: In the plains of Jericho after crossing the Jordan
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:1 — "These are the words of the covenant, which the LORD commanded Moses to make with the children of Israel in the land of Moab, beside the covenant which he made with them in Horeb."

teens.question: Why did Moses say Israel had not yet had "a heart to perceive, and eyes to see, and ears to hear" despite all they had witnessed?
teens.option_a: Because God had not yet granted them spiritual understanding — knowing the signs was not the same as inwardly comprehending God
teens.option_b: Because Israel had been too young during the exodus to understand
teens.option_c: Because the law had not yet been fully given until this moment
teens.option_d: Because sin had permanently blinded their minds
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:4 — "Yet the LORD hath not given you an heart to perceive, and eyes to see, and ears to hear, unto this day." This verse implies that perception itself is a divine gift, not merely a human capacity.

teens.question: Who were included in the covenant renewal in Moab beyond the warriors and leaders?
teens.option_a: All Israel — leaders, tribes, elders, officers, children, wives, the stranger, and the woodcutter and water drawer
teens.option_b: Only adult male Israelites above twenty years
teens.option_c: Israelites plus the two and a half tribes of Transjordan only
teens.option_d: All circumcised males and their firstborn sons
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:10-11 — "Ye stand this day all of you before the LORD your God; your captains of your tribes, your elders, and your officers, with all the men of Israel, Your little ones, your wives, and thy stranger that is in thy camp, from the hewer of thy wood unto the drawer of thy water."

teens.question: What warning did Moses give about a person who thought they could sin and be safe because of the covenant?
teens.option_a: The LORD would not spare him — God's anger and every curse would fall on that man and his name would be blotted out
teens.option_b: He would be expelled from the congregation immediately
teens.option_c: He would be given one chance to repent before the assembly
teens.option_d: The covenant itself would be voided for the whole nation because of him
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:19-20 — "when he heareth the words of this curse, that he bless himself in his heart, saying, I shall have peace, though I walk in the imagination of mine heart...The LORD will not spare him...but the anger of the LORD and his jealousy shall smoke against that man, and all the curses...shall lie upon him."

teens.question: What devastating image did Moses use to describe the fate of the land if Israel abandoned God for idols?
teens.option_a: The land would be as sulphur and salt — burning, like the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah
teens.option_b: The land would become desert and dust with no rain for forty years
teens.option_c: The rivers would dry up and the trees wither from their roots
teens.option_d: Every city would fall into ruins and be left uninhabited
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:23 — "And that the whole land thereof is brimstone, and salt, and burning, that it is not sown, nor beareth, nor any grass groweth therein, like the overthrow of Sodom, and Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboim, which the LORD overthrew in his anger, and in his wrath."

adults.question: What does the covenant in Deuteronomy 29:1 being made "besides the covenant...at Horeb" reveal about the nature of covenant in the Old Testament?
adults.option_a: Covenant in the Old Testament was not a single static transaction but an ongoing, layered relationship that God renewed and deepened through successive historical moments
adults.option_b: The Moab covenant superseded Horeb and the Sinai law was no longer binding
adults.option_c: The Moab covenant was a separate covenant for a separate group of Israelites
adults.option_d: The two covenants applied to different spheres — Horeb to worship, Moab to civil law
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:1 — "beside the covenant which he made with them in Horeb." God did not replace the Horeb covenant but added to it. Covenant renewal is a pattern across Scripture: Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and New Covenant are not contradictions but cumulative developments.

adults.question: How does Paul use Deuteronomy 29:4 in Romans 11:8 to explain Israel's spiritual condition in his own day?
adults.option_a: Paul quotes "God hath given them the spirit of slumber, eyes that they should not see" to explain that Israel's partial hardening was itself a fulfillment of Moses' words, within God's sovereign plan
adults.option_b: Paul uses it to argue that Israel was permanently blinded with no hope of restoration
adults.option_c: Paul uses it to explain that Torah study was insufficient without the Spirit
adults.option_d: Paul quotes it as a condemnation of Israel's hypocrisy rather than a description of divine hardening
adults.explanation: Romans 11:8 — "According as it is written, God hath given them the spirit of slumber, eyes that they should not see, and ears that they should not hear; unto this day." Paul's citation of Deuteronomy 29:4 within his argument for Israel's future restoration shows the hardening is temporary and purposive within God's plan.

adults.question: What does the inclusion of "the hewer of thy wood unto the drawer of thy water" in the covenant assembly communicate about the scope of covenant membership?
adults.option_a: Even the lowest-status workers — possibly Gibeonite servants — were included in the covenant community; covenant membership was not restricted by social class or ethnicity
adults.option_b: The phrase is simply an idiom meaning "everyone without exception" with no literal reference to servants
adults.option_c: It indicated that only workers in the tabernacle service were included
adults.option_d: It was a later addition to the text reflecting post-exilic social realities
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:11 — "from the hewer of thy wood unto the drawer of thy water." Compare Joshua 9:27 — the Gibeonites became "hewers of wood and drawers of water." The covenant at Moab embraced every level of society, anticipating the universal scope of the New Covenant.

adults.question: What theological concept is illustrated by Deuteronomy 29:18's warning about a "root that beareth gall and wormwood" in the midst of Israel?
adults.option_a: The doctrine of corporate corruption — one person's secret apostasy, left unchecked, can poison the entire covenant community
adults.option_b: The verse describes only individual judgment with no communal dimension
adults.option_c: The metaphor refers specifically to Levitical priests who corrupt worship
adults.option_d: The gall and wormwood are purely symbolic of the curses listed in chapter 28
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:18-19 — "Lest there should be among you a root that beareth gall and wormwood; And it come to pass, when he heareth the words of this curse, that he bless himself in his heart." Hebrews 12:15 draws on this verse: "lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled."

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 29:29 function epistemologically — what does it establish about the limits of human knowledge and the nature of revelation?
adults.option_a: It establishes that God's revealed will (the law) is sufficient for human obedience and responsibility, while unrevealed things belong to God's sovereign mystery — we are accountable for what has been revealed, not what has not
adults.option_b: It teaches that the law is incomplete and must be supplemented by prophecy
adults.option_c: It suggests Israel should not speculate about the future at all
adults.option_d: It restricts the law's application to matters Moses explicitly addressed
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:29 — "The secret things belong unto the LORD our God: but those things which are revealed belong unto us and to our children for ever, that we may do all the words of this law." The verse demarcates two domains: divine mystery (not our business) and revealed obligation (our full responsibility).

adults.question: What does Moses' recounting of the wilderness provisions (clothing not wearing out, 29:5) serve as within the covenant renewal ceremony?
adults.option_a: It is a testimony to God's faithfulness designed to evoke gratitude and motivate covenant commitment — "you have seen what God has done; now covenant with him"
adults.option_b: It is historical background with no direct theological connection to the covenant
adults.option_c: It serves to shame Israel for their ingratitude during the wilderness period
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that the wilderness was a period of divine blessing, not judgment
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:2-6 — Moses rehearses the miraculous provisions of the wilderness before calling Israel to covenant. The pattern — recite God's faithfulness, then call to covenant — is the same as in Deuteronomy 1-11 and reflects the covenant treaty structure of historical prologue before stipulations.

adults.question: How does the warning of Deuteronomy 29:19 against the person who "blesseth himself in his heart" connect to the New Testament concept of self-deception?
adults.option_a: It describes the danger of antinomianism — presuming on grace while continuing in sin — which Paul addresses in Romans 6:1 ("Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound?")
adults.option_b: It only applies to flagrant public sinners, not to those who are morally sincere
adults.option_c: It is a warning specifically about false prophets who mislead Israel
adults.option_d: It describes a form of mental illness that the priests were to diagnose and treat
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:19 — "saying, I shall have peace, though I walk in the imagination of mine heart." This is the ancient form of what Paul confronts in Romans 6:1 — the presumption that covenant membership provides immunity from moral accountability. Galatians 6:7 adds: "Be not deceived; God is not mocked."

adults.question: What does the nations' question "Wherefore hath the LORD done thus unto this land?" (29:24-25) and God's answer reveal about the apologetic function of Israel's covenant history?
adults.option_a: Israel's history of blessing and curse was designed to be publicly legible — the nations would be able to read God's justice and faithfulness in Israel's national story
adults.option_b: The question-and-answer format was only meant for internal Israelite catechesis
adults.option_c: The nations' question implies they could not understand God's justice
adults.option_d: The dialogue shows that only Israel would have access to God's explanation
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 29:24-25 — "Even all nations shall say, Wherefore hath the LORD done thus unto this land?...Then men shall say, Because they have forsaken the covenant of the LORD God of their fathers." Israel's national history was meant to be a testimony to God's moral governance before the watching nations.

kids.question: What did Moses say God would do if Israel turned back to him with all their heart after being scattered among the nations?
kids.option_a: God would gather them and bring them back to their land
kids.option_b: God would forgive them but they would stay in exile
kids.option_c: God would send a prophet to lead them out again
kids.option_d: God would give them a new land
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:3 — "That then the LORD thy God will turn thy captivity, and have compassion upon thee, and will return and gather thee from all the nations, whither the LORD thy God hath scattered thee."

kids.question: Moses told the people they had two choices before them — what were they?
kids.option_a: Life and good, or death and evil
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:15 — "See, I have set before thee this day life and good, and death and evil."

kids.question: Moses said the commandment was not too hard or too far away. Where did he say it was?
kids.option_a: In their mouth and in their heart
kids.option_b: Written on the doorpost and the gate
kids.option_c: Kept in the ark of the covenant
kids.option_d: Spoken by the priests each Sabbath
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:14 — "But the word is very nigh unto thee, in thy mouth, and in thy heart, that thou mayest do it."

teens.question: What promise did Moses make about the gathering of scattered Israel — even if they were at the outermost parts of heaven?
teens.option_a: God would fetch them even from the uttermost parts of heaven and bring them back
teens.option_b: They would need to return by their own effort within fifty years
teens.option_c: Only those who remembered the law would be gathered
teens.option_d: The gathering would happen only after a new prophet like Moses arose
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:4 — "If any of thine be driven out unto the outmost parts of heaven, from thence will the LORD thy God gather thee, and from thence will he fetch thee."

teens.question: What did Moses say God would do to Israel's heart and to their enemies' hearts in the time of restoration?
teens.option_a: Circumcise Israel's heart to love God, and put the curses on their enemies
teens.option_b: Renew Israel's spirit and destroy their enemies with fire
teens.option_c: Give Israel a new heart and harden their enemies against them
teens.option_d: Fill Israel's heart with joy and fill their enemies with fear
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:6-7 — "And the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart, and the heart of thy seed, to love the LORD thy God with all thine heart...And the LORD thy God will put all these curses upon thine enemies, and on them that hate thee."

teens.question: What three witnesses did Moses set before Israel when he called them to choose life?
teens.option_a: Heaven and earth, and the word that was near them
teens.option_b: The Levites, the elders, and the congregation
teens.option_c: The blessings, the curses, and the covenant
teens.option_d: Moses, Joshua, and the priests
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:19 — "I call heaven and earth to record this day against you, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life, that both thou and thy seed may live."

teens.question: Why did Moses say the commandment was not in heaven so that one would need to go up and get it?
teens.option_a: Because God had already brought the word near so that it was accessible to every person
teens.option_b: Because the commandment was too deep for human wisdom to understand
teens.option_c: Because only Moses had ascended to heaven to receive it
teens.option_d: Because the law was to be obeyed on earth, not contemplated in heaven
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:12-14 — "It is not in heaven, that thou shouldest say, Who shall go up for us to heaven, and bring it unto us...But the word is very nigh unto thee, in thy mouth, and in thy heart, that thou mayest do it."

teens.question: What three things did Moses say Israel must do to receive the blessings of chapter 30?
teens.option_a: Return to the LORD, obey his voice, and love him with all their heart and soul
teens.option_b: Rebuild the altar, restore the tithes, and renew the circumcision
teens.option_c: Confess their sins, offer sacrifices, and keep the feasts
teens.option_d: Read the law, teach their children, and separate from idolaters
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:2 — "And shalt return unto the LORD thy God, and shalt obey his voice according to all that I command thee this day, thou and thy children, with all thine heart, and with all thy soul."

adults.question: How does Paul apply Deuteronomy 30:12-14 in Romans 10:6-8 to argue for righteousness by faith?
adults.option_a: Paul reinterprets "who shall go up to heaven?" as referring to Christ's incarnation and "who shall descend into the abyss?" as his resurrection, arguing the word of faith is now near in the gospel
adults.option_b: Paul quotes the passage only to show that the Torah was always accessible
adults.option_c: Paul uses it to argue that Gentiles could obey the law without having heard it explicitly
adults.option_d: Paul cites it to prove that salvation was available in the Old Testament without faith in Christ
adults.explanation: Romans 10:6-8 — "But the righteousness which is of faith speaketh on this wise, Say not in thine heart, Who shall ascend into heaven?...Or, Who shall descend into the deep?...But what saith it? The word is nigh thee, even in thy mouth, and in thy heart: that is, the word of faith, which we preach." Paul reads Deuteronomy 30 as prefiguring the nearness of the gospel.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the "circumcision of the heart" promised in Deuteronomy 30:6, and how does it anticipate the New Covenant?
adults.option_a: It promises an inner transformation of the will to love God — pointing toward the New Covenant promise of a new heart (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26) and the Holy Spirit's work
adults.option_b: It refers to the physical sign of circumcision being renewed in the land after exile
adults.option_c: It is a metaphor for intellectual understanding of the law without deeper significance
adults.option_d: It promises that future generations of Israelites would naturally be more obedient
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:6 — "the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart, and the heart of thy seed, to love the LORD thy God." Jeremiah 31:33 — "I will put my law in their inward parts, and write it in their hearts." Ezekiel 36:26 — "A new heart also will I give you." Deuteronomy 30:6 is the seed from which the New Covenant promises grew.

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 30:1-10 function as a hinge between the curses of chapter 28 and the call to choose life in chapter 30:15-20?
adults.option_a: It inserts restoration into the midst of the curse sequence — exile is not the final word; repentance opens the door to gathering, circumcision of heart, and renewed blessing
adults.option_b: It qualifies the curses by limiting them to three generations
adults.option_c: It explains that the curses were conditional while the restoration is unconditional
adults.option_d: It is a parenthetical theological aside not directly connected to chapters 28 or 30:15-20
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:1-3 — "And it shall come to pass, when all these things are come upon thee, the blessing and the curse...And...thou shalt return unto the LORD thy God...That then the LORD thy God will turn thy captivity." The chapter affirms: even the worst-case scenario of covenant curse can be reversed by repentance. Grace is embedded in the curse structure.

adults.question: What does the phrase "I call heaven and earth to record this day against you" (30:19) establish about the cosmic stakes of Israel's covenant choice?
adults.option_a: It establishes heaven and earth as the ultimate judges of Israel's behaviour
adults.option_b: It is a literary device with no more force than a sworn oath before elders
adults.option_c: It implies that the creation itself would be cursed if Israel disobeyed
adults.option_d: The covenant is a transaction witnessed by the entire created order; violation is not merely a personal matter but a breach with cosmic moral significance
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:19 — "I call heaven and earth to record this day against you." In ancient suzerain treaties, gods and cosmic phenomena were invoked as witnesses. Moses invokes the whole creation as witness because the covenant God made is the Creator of all. The choice has ultimate significance.

adults.question: What does Deuteronomy 30:9 promise about Israel's prosperity in restoration compared to what is described in chapter 28, and what condition is attached?
adults.option_a: God will make Israel abundantly prosperous in every work of hands, offspring, and harvest — the reversal of every curse — on the condition that they return to him with all their heart
adults.option_b: The restoration would be gradual and partial, proportionate to Israel's obedience
adults.option_c: The blessing would exceed chapter 28's curses but fall short of the original promises to Abraham
adults.option_d: The condition was only national repentance, not individual heart change
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:9-10 — "And the LORD thy God will make thee plenteous in every work of thine hand...for the LORD will again rejoice over thee for good...If thou shalt hearken unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to keep his commandments and his statutes...and if thou turn unto the LORD thy God with all thine heart."

adults.question: How does the "choose life" command of Deuteronomy 30:19 interact with the sovereign "circumcision of the heart" in 30:6 — is this a tension between divine sovereignty and human responsibility?
adults.option_a: Both are true simultaneously: God sovereignly enables obedience by circumcising the heart while genuinely calling humans to choose — Scripture holds both without resolving the tension
adults.option_b: The sovereign heart change precedes and nullifies the need for human choice
adults.option_c: Human choice precedes the divine heart change as its cause
adults.option_d: The two verses describe different eras: 30:19 is for Moses' generation and 30:6 is for the eschatological age
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:6 — God will circumcise the heart. Deuteronomy 30:19 — "therefore choose life." The text does not resolve the tension but holds both: God is the ultimate cause of true obedience (30:6) and yet the human being is genuinely summoned to choose (30:19). This is the biblical pattern throughout Scripture (Philippians 2:12-13).

adults.question: What does the restoration motif in Deuteronomy 30:3-5 contribute to the theology of the Prophets, particularly Isaiah 40-55 and Ezekiel 36-37?
adults.option_a: Deuteronomy 30 establishes the theological template — gather from exile, circumcise the heart, restore to the land — that Isaiah and Ezekiel develop into the full New Exodus and New Covenant theology
adults.option_b: Isaiah and Ezekiel independently developed restoration theology without drawing on Deuteronomy 30
adults.option_c: The prophets reinterpreted Deuteronomy 30's promises as purely spiritual with no territorial dimension
adults.option_d: Deuteronomy 30 describes only the return from Babylon; the prophets extend it to a future beyond that return
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:3-5 — "gather thee from all the nations...and bring thee into the land." Isaiah 43:5-6 — "I will bring thy seed from the east, and gather thee from the west." Ezekiel 36:24-27 — "I will take you from among the heathen, and gather you...and give you an heart of flesh." The prophets expand what Deuteronomy seeds.

adults.question: How does Deuteronomy 30 as a whole function as the theological heart of the Mosaic covenant and the basis for New Testament soteriology?
adults.option_a: It promises that God himself will ultimately solve the problem of Israel's hard heart — not through stricter law enforcement but through inward transformation — which Paul identifies as accomplished through Christ and the Spirit
adults.option_b: It simply repeats the covenant conditions without adding theological depth
adults.option_c: Its promise of heart circumcision was fully accomplished in the return from Babylon with no further fulfilment needed
adults.option_d: Its primary function is historical, describing what happened after exile without theological import for the New Testament
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 30:6 — "the LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart." Romans 2:29 — "he is a Jew, which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit." Romans 10:8 — "the word is nigh thee." The entire chapter is an Old Testament promise that the Mosaic law's ultimate goal — love of God with the whole heart — would be achieved not by law but by divine transformation, fulfilled in Christ and the Spirit (Romans 8:3-4).

kids.question: How old was Moses when he spoke his farewell to Israel in Deuteronomy 31?
kids.option_a: One hundred and twenty years old
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:2 — "I am an hundred and twenty years old this day; I can no more go out and come in."

kids.question: Who did Moses say would lead Israel into the Promised Land after him?
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:3 — "The LORD thy God, he will go over before thee, and he will destroy these nations from before thee, and thou shalt possess them: and Joshua, he shall go over before thee."

kids.question: Where did Moses tell the priests to put the book of the law?
kids.option_a: In the side of the ark of the covenant
kids.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:26 — "Take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against thee."

teens.question: What did Moses tell the people not to be afraid of, and why?
teens.option_a: He told them not to fear the nations of Canaan because God would go before them
teens.option_b: He told them not to fear the wilderness because water would be provided
teens.option_c: He told them not to fear Pharaoh because he was already defeated
teens.option_d: He told them not to fear death because Moses would pray for them
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:6 — "Be strong and of a good courage, fear not, nor be afraid of them: for the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee."

teens.question: What did God say the Israelites would do after Moses died?
teens.option_a: They would forsake God and follow other gods of the land
teens.option_b: They would build a great temple to the LORD
teens.option_c: They would return to Egypt immediately
teens.option_d: They would divide the land and live in peace forever
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:16 — "This people will rise up, and go a whoring after the gods of the strangers of the land, whither they go to be among them, and will forsake me, and break my covenant which I have made with them."

teens.question: What was the purpose of the song God commanded Moses to write?
teens.option_a: To be a witness against Israel when they sinned
teens.option_b: To praise God for the Exodus from Egypt
teens.option_c: To teach the law to future generations
teens.option_d: To honor Joshua as the new leader
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:19 — "Now therefore write ye this song for you, and teach it the children of Israel: put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel."

teens.question: How often were the priests commanded to read the law to all Israel?
teens.option_a: Every seven years at the feast of tabernacles
teens.option_b: Every year at Passover
teens.option_c: Every Sabbath in the tabernacle
teens.option_d: Every jubilee year
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:10-11 — "At the end of every seven years...in the feast of tabernacles...thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing."

teens.question: What did God say would happen to Israel in the latter days?
teens.option_a: Evil would befall them because they would turn from God
teens.option_b: They would rule all surrounding nations
teens.option_c: God would give them rest from all enemies
teens.option_d: They would return to Egypt and be delivered again
teens.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:29 — "For I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves, and turn aside from the way which I have commanded you; and evil will befall you in the latter days."

adults.question: What specific phrase did Moses use to describe his inability to lead Israel, and what reason did he give?
adults.option_a: He said he could "no more go out and come in" because God had said he would not cross Jordan
adults.option_b: He said he was too old to command the army and would rest in his tent
adults.option_c: He said his eyesight had failed and he could not see the path forward
adults.option_d: He said his strength had left him and he could no longer speak plainly
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:2 — "I am an hundred and twenty years old this day; I can no more go out and come in: also the LORD hath said unto me, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan."

adults.question: What did God tell Moses He would do to the nations of Canaan, using what imagery?
adults.option_a: God said He would destroy them and Moses used the example of what He did to Sihon and Og
adults.option_b: God said He would scatter them as He scattered the builders of Babel
adults.option_c: God said He would send hornets before Israel as He did in Egypt
adults.option_d: God said He would dry up their rivers as He dried up the Red Sea
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:4 — "And the LORD shall do unto them as he did to Sihon and to Og, kings of the Amorites, and unto the land of them, whom he destroyed."

adults.question: What was the setting in which Moses formally commissioned Joshua before all Israel?
adults.option_a: Moses called Joshua and said "Be strong and of a good courage" before all Israel in a public charge
adults.option_b: Moses laid hands on Joshua privately in the tabernacle
adults.option_c: Moses wrote Joshua's name in the book of the law as successor
adults.option_d: Moses anointed Joshua with oil at the altar of burnt offering
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:7 — "And Moses called unto Joshua, and said unto him in the sight of all Israel, Be strong and of a good courage."

adults.question: What appearance did God make regarding Joshua's commissioning, and where did it take place?
adults.option_a: The LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud over the door, and commissioned Joshua there
adults.option_b: The LORD appeared on Mount Sinai with thunder and lightning as He did before Moses
adults.option_c: The LORD appeared in fire on the altar and spoke from the flame
adults.option_d: The LORD appeared to Joshua alone in a dream that night after Moses died
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:15 — "And the LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud: and the pillar of the cloud stood over the door of the tabernacle."

adults.question: What theological concept did God announce about His future relationship with Israel after their apostasy?
adults.option_a: God said He would hide His face from them, and they would be devoured by evils and troubles
adults.option_b: God said He would send them prophets to warn them before any punishment
adults.option_c: God said He would reduce them to a small remnant but never destroy them entirely
adults.option_d: God said He would give them over to their enemies for forty years as in the wilderness
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:17-18 — "Then my anger shall be kindled against them in that day, and I will forsake them, and I will hide my face from them...and I will surely hide my face in that day for all the evils which they shall have wrought."

adults.question: What was the role of the Levites specified in connection with the book of the law?
adults.option_a: They bore the ark of the covenant and were to take the book of the law and place it beside the ark
adults.option_b: They were to memorize the entire book of the law and recite it annually
adults.option_c: They were to make copies of the law for each of the twelve tribes
adults.option_d: They were to teach the law in each city gate every Sabbath day
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:25-26 — "That Moses commanded the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, saying, Take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark."

adults.question: What were the "elders of the tribes" and "officers" summoned to hear at the end of Moses' ministry?
adults.option_a: Moses spoke the words of the song in their ears and called heaven and earth to record against Israel
adults.option_b: Moses recited the entire law from beginning to end one final time
adults.option_c: Moses announced the boundaries of each tribe's inheritance in Canaan
adults.option_d: Moses gave final instructions for the building of the temple
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 31:28-30 — "Gather unto me all the elders of your tribes, and your officers, that I may speak these words in their ears...and Moses spake in the ears of all the congregation of Israel the words of this song."

adults.question: What striking contrast did Moses draw between the present day and the future in his final warning?
adults.option_a: Moses said they were rebellious even while he was alive with them, so much more after his death
adults.option_b: Moses said they had been faithful in the wilderness but would fail in the prosperity of Canaan
adults.option_c: Moses said the children would be more faithful than their parents had been
adults.option_d: Moses said the Promised Land itself would cause them to forget God because of its abundance
